title: Microsoft links Mastra AI supply chain attack to North Korean hackers
blurb: Microsoft attributed the Mastra AI supply chain attack, which compromised over 140 npm packages in the @mastra scope, to North Korean state actor Sapphire Sleet. Attackers hijacked a maintainer account to inject a dayjs typosquat that ran a post-install dropper, deploying a cross-platform infostealer that targeted credentials, API keys, and 166 crypto wallet browser extensions across Windows, macOS, and Linux.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after roughly 74,000 firewall and VPN credentials were exposed in a leak dubbed "FortiBleed." Researcher Volodymyr Diachenko found a server holding plaintext passwords for 73,932 firewall URLs spanning 21,632 domains across 194 countries, including Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Chevron, AT&T, and Toyota, with attackers already using the credentials against internet-facing devices.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift published usbliter8, a working exploit that achieves arbitrary code execution inside the SecureROM of Apple's A12 and A13 chips by abusing a flaw in the Synopsys DWC2 USB controller. Because the code is burned into silicon, no software update can fix it, though the attack needs physical access to a device in DFU mode and finishes in under two seconds.

title: Bun has an open PR adding shared-memory threads to JavaScriptCore
blurb: Bun creator Jarred Sumner has an open PR adding shared-memory threads to JavaScriptCore, where `new Thread(fn, ...args)` runs a real closure on another core inside the same heap, with no structured clone, message passing, or blob-URL worker hacks. Parallel JavaScript already executes across all four JIT tiers with no global lock and the thread test suite passes, but the author cautions the work is experimental and may never merge.

title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
blurb: Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain that turns an AI browsing agent into a path to remote code execution by steering it to a malicious web page whose JavaScript reaches a privileged local service and spawns a host process. The flaw lives in AutoGen Studio's MCP WebSocket handler, which trusted localhost, skipped authentication, and ran request-supplied commands; it never shipped in a stable PyPI release but did reach two unyanked pre-release builds.

title: Intel and AMD's new ACE CPU extensions bring an efficient AI-oriented instruction set to x86 — a new design makes matrix multiplication more power- and density-efficient
blurb: Intel and AMD released the full specification for ACE, a shared x86 instruction set that adds dedicated matrix-multiplication silicon on top of existing AVX10 registers to run AI workloads on the CPU more power-efficiently. ACE performs up to 16x as many operations per input vector as AVX10 and is implementation-agnostic, letting frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow target one code path instead of vendor-specific variants.

title: MolmoMotion: Language-guided 3D motion forecasting
blurb: AI2 released MolmoMotion, a model that forecasts an object's 3D point trajectory from a video frame and a written action instruction.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Simon Willison released datasette-apps, a plugin that hosts self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside Datasette, running them in a tightly constrained iframe sandbox that can issue read-only SQL queries (and writes through configured stored queries). The sandbox blocks cookie and localStorage access and injects a CSP header preventing outbound HTTP requests, so a buggy or malicious app cannot exfiltrate private data.

title: Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems
blurb: Martin Fowler's site published a case study on a system built with Bayer that lets pharmaceutical researchers query decades of study data buried in PDF reports. Author Sarang Sanjay Kulkarni traces its evolution from keyword search to an agentic research assistant that answers complex questions and drafts regulatory documents, detailing the reliability engineering required to make LLM outputs trustworthy.

title: Alice is impatient
blurb: This post explains the inspection paradox: your customers experience a time-weighted version of your latency distribution, so they feel far slower service than your averages report. A user perceives mean latency as E[X²]/E[X], or E[X] plus variance over the mean, meaning a service with a 30-minute median TTR and rare 10-hour tails shows a 1-hour MTTR while customers experience closer to 6 hours.

title: AWS Plugs Coinbase's x402 Into CloudFront, Letting Publishers Charge AI Agents in USDC
blurb: AWS turned on AI traffic monetization inside AWS WAF, letting any site behind CloudFront charge AI agents per request in stablecoins through Coinbase's x402 protocol. The feature returns an HTTP 402 with a price manifest, settles USDC on Base or Solana via Coinbase's Facilitator at no extra cost to CloudFront customers, and marks the first time a hyperscale cloud has wired onchain settlement directly into its content-delivery edge.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards integration after detecting unusual activity that exposed a subset of customer data, the result of an extortion group called Icarus compromising the competitive-intelligence vendor on June 11. Attackers used a leaked legacy credential to steal OAuth tokens connecting Klue to platforms like Salesforce, then accessed connected environments including those of cybersecurity firm Huntress, exposing business contacts and sales data.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through infected USB drives, targeting Windows users since February. The worm, identified as Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits, monitors the clipboard for seed phrases and private keys, exfiltrates them over Tor, and silently swaps in attacker-controlled addresses during transfers.

title: There are no instances in ATProto
blurb: Dan Abramov argues that asking "where are the Bluesky instances?" is a category error, because atproto has no instances the way Mastodon does. He frames atproto as a return to the RSS-era split between hosting and aggregation, where your data lives in your own repository and apps are interchangeable projections over the network rather than walled communities you join.

title: Researchers drop checkm8-style BootROM exploit for A12 and A13 iPhones
blurb: Researchers released a checkm8-style BootROM exploit targeting iPhones built on Apple's A12 and A13 chips, the silicon behind the iPhone XS through iPhone 11 lines. Because the flaw sits in unpatchable read-only boot code, Apple cannot fix it in software, giving forensic tools and jailbreakers persistent low-level access on affected devices that requires physical possession to exploit.

title: Project Valhalla, Explained: How a Decade of Work Arrives in JDK 28
blurb: Oracle confirmed that JEP 401: Value Classes and Objects will land in JDK 28, marking the first piece of Project Valhalla to reach the main OpenJDK repository after a decade of work. The change ships as a disabled-by-default preview spanning 197,000 lines across 1,816 files, delivering Valhalla's "codes like a class, works like an int" goal while leaving the rest of the project for future releases.

title: Apple is about to make Hide My Email useless
blurb: Apple is moving all Sign in with Apple and Hide My Email aliases to a dedicated @private.icloud.com subdomain, making it trivial for services to identify and block relay addresses without affecting regular iCloud mailboxes. The change preserves the carve-out that gave the feature its value, since aliases were previously hard to ban without hitting legitimate iCloud users, and could push many sites to reject them outright like disposable email.

title: Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment
blurb: OpenAI shared a method for predicting how a model will behave in production by simulating its deployment before release. The approach runs candidate models through reconstructed real-world scenarios to surface failures and unsafe responses early, aiming to catch problems that standard benchmarks miss before customers ever touch the system.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco will remove VMware from its fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known migration off the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition. The shift follows a 175% VMware price hike and Tesco's $134M lawsuit alleging Broadcom broke perpetual licenses, part of a broader enterprise exodus as Broadcom pushes customers onto pricier subscriptions and pulls support from holdouts.

title: Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise
blurb: Anthropic released an analysis of ~400,000 Claude Code sessions from October 2025 to April 2026, finding that users set the plan while Claude handles execution, and that every major occupation completes coding tasks at nearly the same success rate as software engineers. Over the seven months, debugging's share of sessions fell by half, usage shifted toward end-to-end agentic work, and the estimated value of a typical task rose about 25%.

title: John Jumper to join Anthropic
blurb: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind to join Anthropic after nearly nine years, following a planned break to recharge. Jumper led the AlphaFold protein-structure team that won him the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, and his move adds a marquee scientific researcher to Anthropic as frontier labs compete for top AI talent.

title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
blurb: Apple patched a high-severity Beats Studio Buds flaw (CVE-2025-20701) that let attackers within Bluetooth range eavesdrop through the microphone before pairing.

title: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after blockbuster IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after its blockbuster IPO, to shore up its struggling AI division. The all-stock deal follows SpaceX telling IPO investors it sees a $26 trillion addressable market in AI, a bet that pairs Cursor's coding tools with the rocket maker's expanding software ambitions.

title: Linux eliminates the strncpy API after six years of work, 360 patches
blurb: The Linux kernel has fully eliminated the strncpy() API in version 7.2, completing a six-year effort spanning roughly 362 commits to remove every remaining caller and the last per-architecture implementations. The function was a long-deprecated source of bugs due to counterintuitive NUL-termination semantics and wasteful zero-filling, and callers are now directed to replacements like strscpy(), strscpy_pad(), strtomem_pad(), or memcpy().

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, according to SOCRadar researchers who found the attackers' exposed server. The campaign relied on credential harvesting rather than a Fortinet flaw, targeting default, built-in, and long-lived accounts across telecom, government, healthcare, and financial sectors, with India and the US accounting for nearly a third of confirmed compromises.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and tested on Pixel 6a, 7, 7a, 8, and the 10 series, with the first official release due a day after Android 17's launch. The privacy-focused Android fork is shipping a final Android 16 QPR2 build alongside the new port, and public testing across all supported devices begins immediately.

title: AI coding agents taught robots how to install GPUs and cut zip ties
blurb: Researchers at Nvidia's GEAR lab, Carnegie Mellon, and UC Berkeley built ENPIRE, an agent harness that lets AI coding agents autonomously train robots to cut zip ties and insert GPUs into tight motherboard sockets. Tested with OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, and Moonshot's Kimi Code, the system runs robots in parallel overnight, analyzes failure logs, and refines its own training algorithms, with the team planning to open-source everything.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney announced its first hardware product, an ultrasound machine, marking an unexpected pivot from AI image generation into personal health and medical devices. The company revealed the project at a San Francisco event, signaling ambitions beyond the consumer image tools that built its reputation as it enters a heavily regulated industry dominated by established medical-imaging firms.

title: Introducing AWS Continuum for security at machine speed
blurb: AWS announced AWS Continuum, a security service that discovers, validates, and remediates code vulnerabilities at machine speed within customer-defined guardrails, now in gated preview. The system ingests findings from existing tools, builds reproducible exploit proofs in an isolated sandbox, and applies reversible mitigations before routing durable fixes through the customer's own review process, shifting security teams from manual triage to approving outcomes.

title: Google workspace threatening to block Firefox access
blurb: Google Workspace began showing Firefox users a warning that they must download Chrome to keep accessing their work accounts, surfacing the prompt on a Business Plus admin login from an up-to-date browser. Google support told the reporting user the message is only a recommendation for admins reaching admin.google.com rather than a block, but said it would not document the behavior publicly.

title: How we run Firecracker VMs inside EC2 and start browsers in less than 1s
blurb: Browser Use rebuilt its cloud browser infrastructure on Firecracker microVMs running inside EC2, cutting session cost from $0.06 to $0.02 per browser hour and starting new browsers in under a second. Each session gets its own VM for isolation, replacing a Unikraft unikernel setup that handled idle shutdown well but couldn't autoscale fast enough during traffic spikes.

title: Cell-Based Architecture for Resilient Payment Systems
blurb: American Express detailed the cell-based architecture behind its core payments platform, which groups microservices, databases, and components into independent cells that contain failures within a single boundary. The design shrinks the blast radius of localized faults while preserving low-latency processing and letting the company scale capacity without enlarging the failure domain.

title: Building AI Agents for AR Glasses and XR Devices with NVIDIA XR AI
blurb: NVIDIA released XR AI in public beta, an open-source library for building AI agents that run on AR glasses and XR headsets by routing live camera and microphone streams to GPU-accelerated cloud or edge models. The agents combine visual grounding via Cosmos, voice interaction through Nemotron, and enterprise tool use over MCP, with early deployments at Siemens factories and Stanford and Princeton research labs.

title: Loupe – A iOS app that raises awareness about what native apps can see
blurb: Loupe is an open-source iOS app that shows you the raw device-fingerprinting data any third-party app can quietly read.

title: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving DeepMind for rival Anthropic
blurb: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind for rival Anthropic after nearly nine years, where he led the AlphaFold team that won him the 2024 chemistry prize. His exit lands alongside Noam Shazeer's departure to OpenAI, marking a fresh wave of senior talent flowing out of DeepMind to competing frontier labs.

title: The 100k Whys of AI
blurb: The author argues that AI text is detectable not because each model has unique tics, but because all of them fall back on the same complex set of mannerisms for almost any prompt. He illustrates this with roughly 150 nearly identical Amazon book covers returned for "100,000 whys," showing how quasi-deterministic tools produce functionally identical output around 80% of the time when given similar prompts.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco announced it will acquire identity lifecycle security startup WideField Security to deepen the identity and session intelligence inside Splunk's Agentic SOC. The deal, whose terms were not disclosed, folds WideField's identity discovery, exposure mapping, and live session monitoring into Cisco's data fabric and marks the company's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix Security.

title: PostgresBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Postgres Services
blurb: ClickHouse released PostgresBench, a public and reproducible benchmark for comparing managed Postgres services using pgbench and its standard TPC-B-like transactional workload. It applies the same open methodology as ClickHouse's ClickBench OLAP benchmark, publishing all configuration and queries so anyone can validate results, submit their own, or flag unfair setups.

title: New attack turned Microsoft 365 Copilot into 1-click data theft tool
blurb: Researchers chained three flaws into SearchLeak, a critical Microsoft 365 Copilot bug that exfiltrated mailbox and SharePoint data from one click; Microsoft has patched it.

title: US energy regulator to order grid operators to expedite AI data center applications — says projects should bring their own power or cut usage during high demand
blurb: The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is preparing an order requiring grid operators to fast-track AI data center connections, prioritizing projects that bring their own power or curtail demand during peak stress. The move backs Trump's AI Action Plan and comes as data centers strain the grid, with largest operator PJM Interconnection raising power costs 75.5% in an increase largely blamed on AI buildout.

title: AI coding agents may be getting bad instructions from ‘smelly’ config files
blurb: Researchers at Brazil's Federal University of Minas Gerais published the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, finding at least one flaw in 91 of 100 popular open-source repositories. The most common are lint leakage (62%), context bloat (42%), and skill leakage (35%), structural issues that bloat context, waste tokens, and make agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex less reliable.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: Amazon is in early talks to sell its homegrown Trainium AI chips to outside companies for use in their own data centers, AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg. CEO Andy Jassy has pegged the chip business at a ~$50B run rate, a figure that rivals Intel's revenue but still trails Nvidia's $326B, and any external sales would force AWS to ration capacity that already sells out faster than it can produce.

title: Companies rein in AI usage as costs strain budgets
blurb: Companies are scaling back AI deployments as inference and licensing costs outpace the budgets set during last year's adoption rush, according to the FT. The shift suggests the spending bottleneck has moved from proving AI works to making it pay off, with finance teams now scrutinizing per-query costs that engineering enthusiasm initially obscured.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The Model Context Protocol's Enterprise-Managed Authorization extension is now stable, letting organizations centrally control MCP server access through their existing identity provider instead of per-user OAuth. Employees get zero-touch setup where needed servers connect on first login, with the client exchanging an ID-JAG token from the IdP for server access tokens. Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta are among early adopters.

title: [AINews] GLM > GPT? GLM-5.2 passes vibe check; Z.ai forecasts Open Fable by December
blurb: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 is drawing consensus as the first open-weight model to credibly reach the frontier, with Artificial Analysis' knowledge-work benchmark scoring it above GPT-5.5 and practitioners on r/LocalLlama and elsewhere echoing the result. The validation marks Z.ai as a genuine frontier lab and sets up a December timeline for an open Fable-class model, narrowing the gap between open releases and the top four closed labs.

title: From PGP to Mythos: a brief history of export controls that didn’t stop anyone
blurb: The author argues that the White House's order forcing Anthropic to restrict exports of its Fable and Mythos models echoes decades of failed attempts to contain cyber technology through export controls, from 1990s encryption to commercial spyware. History suggests the rules rarely stop proliferation and instead reshape which markets labs can reach, making this standoff a template every frontier AI lab will have to build around.

title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 26H2, urges IT admins to prepare for release
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 version 26H2 and is urging IT admins to begin preparing for its release, expected to roll out around early October 2026. The update ships as a small enablement package that toggles features already present in the code base rather than replacing the OS, making deployment quicker, though 26H1 users on the separate scoped branch cannot upgrade to it.

title: UHF X11: X11 Built for VisionOS and Apple Vision Pro
blurb: UHF X11 turns Apple Vision Pro into a full X11 display server, opening each X11 top-level as its own rootless visionOS spatial window placed anywhere in your space. Trusted machines connect over native X11 TCP with on-device auth cookies, GLX clients render OpenGL through X11, and CRT presets add scanlines, phosphor masks, and glow for vintage display character.

title: AI Coding Agent Horror Stories: The 13-Hour AWS Outage
blurb: Docker's blog recounts how an AI coding agent triggered a 13-hour AWS outage, the latest entry in a growing catalog of autonomous-agent failures in production. The post argues that as agents gain authority to execute infrastructure changes, the cost of an unsupervised mistake scales with their access, making sandboxing and human approval gates the practical guardrails rather than optional extras.

title: Tensordyne makes a big bet on log math to beat Nvidia
blurb: Tensordyne is betting on logarithmic number systems to undercut Nvidia, replacing the compute-hungry multiplications in AI workloads with cheaper additions of logarithms. The startup claims the approach delivers competitive performance per watt for inference, though it still has to prove the math holds up against entrenched GPU tooling and Nvidia's software ecosystem.

title: Mastodon 4.6 adds profile Collections and two-factor controls
blurb: Mastodon 4.6 adds Collections for sharing curated profile lists and gives server admins a switch to require two-factor authentication.

title: Acoustic mapping app uses thousands of networked old Android phones to hunt Shahed drones — crowd-sourced microphone network spots small, low-RCS military targets
blurb: A Lithuanian startup built an Android app that turns thousands of networked old phones into a crowd-sourced microphone array for detecting Shahed-type attack drones. Verified users run an algorithm that isolates the drones' acoustic signature from ambient noise and plots possible sightings on a public map, offering a cheap way to catch small, low-RCS targets that radar struggles to distinguish from clutter like birds.

title: Slow breathing modulates brain function and risk behavior
blurb: A new study in Neuron reports that slow breathing measurably shifts brain activity and reduces risk-taking behavior in human subjects. The authors link the effect to changes in prefrontal regions tied to decision-making, suggesting that respiratory rhythm directly modulates cognition rather than just reflecting emotional state.

title: CSSQuake
blurb: CSSQuake is a browser-based Quake-style first-person shooter rendered almost entirely with CSS rather than WebGL or canvas. The project supports multiplayer matches, dynamic lighting, particles, and configurable gameplay settings, demonstrating how far the modern CSS rendering pipeline can be pushed for real-time 3D.

title: Lithuanian startup launches open-source network to detect Shahed-type drones
blurb: Lithuanian startup Mainline launched Drone Radar, an open-source network that detects Shahed-type drones by running an app on volunteers' unused Android phones to listen for the low-frequency acoustic signatures of drone engines. Overlapping detections from nearby devices triangulate a drone's location on a public map, and the team aims to recruit 10,000 participants across the Baltics and Poland, with plans to add cell-tower and surveillance-camera sensors.

title: Hey, n00b, we didn't hire you to complete tasks
blurb: Kent Beck argues that junior engineers aren't hired to complete tasks, since a tech lead could finish the same work faster, but as an "option premium" on the senior they may become. He frames a junior's real job as sorting themselves into the top tier by signaling growth, because seniors triage their limited mentoring time toward the people most likely to make it.

title: LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers
blurb: Researchers at Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability chain in LiteLLM, the open-source AI gateway that brokers calls to over 100 model providers, letting a default low-privilege user escalate to admin and run code on the server. The CVSS 9.9 chain exposes every provider key, credential-decryption secret, and prompt passing through the proxy; maintainer BerriAI shipped the full fix in v1.83.14-stable, so upgrade to that release or later.

title: NASA picks Eric Schmidt’s rocket company for Mars mission, setting up a race with SpaceX
blurb: NASA hired Relativity Space, the rocket maker Eric Schmidt acquired last year, to build and fly the Aeolus orbiter to Mars on a 2028 launch timeline. The mission's four instruments will deliver the first daily global view of Martian dust, winds, and temperature, using the same low-cost public-private contract model NASA pioneered with SpaceX for ISS cargo runs.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer to join IPO-bound OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-lead of Google's Gemini and a co-author of the original Transformer paper, is leaving Google to join OpenAI as it prepares for an IPO. The hire deepens OpenAI's research bench ahead of a public offering and marks another high-profile defection from Google's flagship AI effort, intensifying the talent war between the two labs.

title: Framer 3.0
blurb: Framer 3.0 adds Agents that design directly on the canvas, building entire pages, creating components, writing code, connecting to the CMS, and managing styles and breakpoints. The release also introduces Branching for teams to iterate and adopt Agents safely, plus a Community where creators can share and earn from their work.

title: Operation Endgame Disrupts SocGholish Servers, Cleans 14,971 WordPress Sites
blurb: Operation Endgame disrupted SocGholish, taking down 106 servers and cleaning 14,971 infected WordPress sites across four countries.

title: Identity verification on Claude
blurb: Anthropic is rolling out identity verification on Claude, requiring a government-issued photo ID and a live selfie to access certain capabilities, run platform integrity checks, and meet compliance obligations. The company partnered with Persona to collect and store the ID and selfie data, which Anthropic says it uses only to confirm identity and not for other purposes.

title: Hackers hijacked Brazil’s emergency alert system and sent ‘misanthropy’ to millions of phones
blurb: Hackers breached Brazil's national civil defense alert system overnight, pushing at least 10 fake "Extreme Alert" notifications reading "Defesa Civil: misantropi4" to phones across seven states before the platform was taken offline at 1:30 am Saturday. The messages used the most severe alert category, which bypasses silent mode and is reserved for imminent disasters, reaching an estimated 30 million people and prompting a Federal Police investigation.

title: Microsoft’s June update fixed 208 security flaws and introduced a cascade of new bugs across all Windows versions
blurb: Microsoft's June Patch Tuesday update KB5094126 fixed a record 208 security vulnerabilities but broke core functionality across every supported version of Windows, including Recycle Bin deletion dialogs, OneDrive on domain PCs, and BitLocker on enterprise hardware. Affected machines including HP EliteBook and Dell Precision laptops are rebooting into recovery screens, and Microsoft says the first fix will not ship until July 14.

title: Perplexity releases Brain Memory System for Perplexity Computer
blurb: Perplexity released Brain, a shared memory system that sits beneath products like Perplexity Search and its Computer agent rather than living inside any one of them. Unlike most opaque assistant memory, Brain exposes the full knowledge base to users as categorized topics, the context behind each, and a navigable 3D map of their connections, and it pulls context only when a task needs it.

title: NASA's Swift Boost mission will launch later this month to rescue a falling telescope
blurb: NASA's Swift Boost mission is set to launch June 27 to rescue the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory, whose orbit is decaying faster than expected due to increased solar activity and atmospheric drag. A robotic spacecraft called LINK, built with Arizona's Katalyst Space, will dock with the 2004-era gamma-ray telescope and tug it to a higher orbit, extending its life by several years.

title: Car manufacturers are ditching Android Auto in 2026: Here's why
blurb: GM is dropping Android Auto from its EVs and plans to remove it across its lineup, replacing the projection system with an in-house infotainment platform built on Google's Gemini AI. The shift is driven by subscription revenue, vehicle data the automaker can monetize, and control over the dashboard, suggesting more manufacturers may follow even though most 2026 models still support the feature.

title: Microsoft will soon allow some users to block Copilot from analyzing their Office files
blurb: Microsoft is updating a Purview sensitivity setting to let commercial customers block Copilot and other connected AI tools from analyzing labeled Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files. The change, tagged MC1297982, enforces blocking across all connected services and applies automatically to already-labeled files, with phased general availability completing by the end of next month for customers on qualifying Purview licenses.

title: Beyond Siri: Here are the practical AI features coming to your iPhone in iOS 27
blurb: Apple is embedding Apple Intelligence into core iOS 27 apps rather than routing everything through its overhauled Siri, adding features like photo-based restaurant bill splitting via Apple Cash, automatic password updates after breaches, and task automation. The features are live in the developer beta now and reach the public beta ahead of iOS 27's general release this fall.

title: China unifies tech sector to build grid-free orbiting satellite AI data centers, challenging Elon Musk's SpaceX — Beijing's forced chip and satellite alliance announced a week before Musk’s AI1 reveal
blurb: China's government approved the Space Computing Industry Innovation Center in early June, forcing rocket makers, chip fabs, and AI firms into one alliance to build orbiting, grid-free AI data centers. The state-coordinated approach contrasts with US rivals SpaceX and Blue Origin, which are pursuing space-based compute solo, and lands a week before Elon Musk's AI1 satellite reveal.

title: TechCrunch Mobility: A new robotaxi scorecard shows China’s dominance
blurb: Autnmy AI released its Road to Autonomy Index, a generative-AI benchmarking system that ranks autonomous vehicle companies across robotaxis, licensing, trucks, and delivery bots using public filings, regulatory reports, and SEC data refreshed every 12 hours. As of Friday the robotaxi leader was Baidu's Apollo Go, narrowly ahead of Waymo, with Pony.ai and WeRide next and Tesla in fifth.

title: From $5B to $13B in five months: Baseten reportedly closing in on $1.5B raise
blurb: Baseten is reportedly nearing a $1.5B funding round at up to a $13B valuation, just five months after its $300M Series E valued the AI inference platform at $5B. Prior backers Conviction and Spark Capital are co-leading the split-priced deal, reflecting investor attention shifting from training frontier models to the infrastructure needed to run them in production.

title: Unauthorized alert sent to cell phones across Brazil
blurb: Hackers hijacked Brazil's emergency alert system to blast an "extreme" warning reading "misantropi4" to phones across Paraná, São Paulo, and Rio, forcing the platform offline.

title: Hyundai buys Boston Dynamics
blurb: Hyundai will acquire SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325 million, expected to close June 22 and making the Waltham robotics company a wholly owned subsidiary. The deal completes Hyundai's takeover that began with an $880 million majority purchase in 2021, and positions its factories as the first deployment site for the electric Atlas humanoid, which is slated to start work at a Georgia EV plant by 2028.

title: A new bill takes aim at government pressure to silence lawful online speech
blurb: Senators Ted Cruz and Ron Wyden introduced the bipartisan JAWBONE Act, which would create a federal cause of action against government officials who coerce broadcasters, online platforms, or AI providers into suppressing lawful, First-Amendment-protected speech. The bill also sets up a transparency system for government communications with intermediaries about user expression, giving censored parties a legal right to sue beyond what the First Amendment already provides.

title: White House delays US voting-machine vulnerability report
blurb: The White House delayed the public release of a federal study documenting security vulnerabilities in US voting machines, with the 2026 midterms approaching. The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency completed the report months ago, and election-security researchers argue that withholding the findings leaves states and vendors without time to patch flaws before voters head to the polls.

title: Chinese hackers hijack auth flow, spy on isolated network for a decade
blurb: Chinese hackers hijacked an organization's authentication stack and persisted on an isolated network for a decade with full visibility into admin activity.

title: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of the original Transformer paper and former Character.AI cofounder, announced he is leaving Google to join OpenAI. Shazeer rejoined Google in 2024 as part of the Character.AI deal and had been helping lead Gemini, so his move hands OpenAI a senior architecture mind while removing one from Google's flagship model effort.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is building a full-body ultrasonic scanner that maps the body to a fraction of a millimeter in under 60 seconds, roughly 100 times faster than a full-body MRI. The company is developing the hardware with Butterfly Network under an exclusive license to its ultrasound-on-chip technology, and plans to house the machines in dedicated spas under a new Midjourney Medical division.

title: Who owns your ATProto identity?
blurb: The author argues that on ATProto, your PDS operator holds your signing and rotation keys, meaning they can post, like, follow, and commit as you across every app in the ecosystem, with signatures cryptographically indistinguishable from your own. Because a single repository underpins Bluesky, Tangled, Grain, and Leaflet, one operator can impersonate or lock you out of your entire decentralized identity at once, not just a single platform.

title: Mistral AI to get Code and Apps features on Vibe
blurb: Mistral AI is building Code and Apps sections into the web version of Le Chat (Vibe), adding a browser-based coding surface alongside an artifacts-style area for building, hosting, and sharing apps via connectors and workflows. The features land as CEO Arthur Mensch confirmed a new open-weights model this summer, a large sparse mixture-of-experts design with partner access starting in July, signaling Mistral's push from model lab to full product platform.

title: I hate compilers
blurb: Anubis author Xe Iaso compiled the `wasm2js` tool to WebAssembly and vendored it into the bot-protection system, so its WebAssembly proof-of-work check and its JavaScript fallback produce bit-for-bit identical logic from a single source. Reaching reproducible builds meant fighting three compiler quirks: timestamp macros breaking determinism, Clang silently shelling out to a system `wasm-opt`, and address-sensitive codegen that varied output by ~29 bytes across architectures.

title: GlassWASM: WebAssembly Malware Found in Trojanized Open VSX Extensions
blurb: Socket's threat research team found GlassWASM, a malware campaign hiding compiled WebAssembly payloads inside trojanized VS Code extensions republished on the Open VSX marketplace. The TinyGo-built module encrypts its strings with ChaCha20 and uses the Solana blockchain as a takedown-resistant command-and-control channel, polling for attacker transactions to fetch and run second-stage payloads with no seizable server.

title: Why Amazon hates 'human-in-the-loop' AI governance
blurb: Amazon Security VP Eric Brandwine argues that human-in-the-loop is not the gold standard for AI governance, because humans are non-deterministic, inconsistent, and prone to the "normalization of deviance" that erodes oversight discipline over time. His point is that years of false alarms train human reviewers to stop responding, so layering a human atop an agent often launders accountability rather than adding real control.

title: GLM-5.2 Becomes the Top Open-Weight Model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Artificial Analysis named Z.ai's GLM-5.2 the top open-weight model on its Intelligence Index, scoring 51 under an MIT license.

title: Google DeepMind Explores the Path to ASI
blurb: Google DeepMind released a report mapping the transition from human-level AGI to artificial superintelligence, defining ASI as a system more cognitively capable than large organizations of humans. The paper lays out four possible pathways to ASI, including scaling, recursive self-improvement, and superintelligence emerging from large multi-agent collectives, then catalogs the frictions and open research questions along each route.

title: Google Hits 50% IPv6
blurb: Google's continuous user measurements show IPv6 traffic to its services crossing 50% for the first time, a milestone that marks the protocol as mature and deployed at global scale. Adoption remains uneven across regions, with APNIC Labs measuring 42% worldwide capability and economies like India, Vietnam, and Saudi Arabia following sharply different curves due to differing measurement methods and weighting.

title: Is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
blurb: Meta's engineering culture has unraveled in just weeks under AI-driven leadership pressure, according to a Pragmatic Engineer report from Gergely Orosz. The piece details engineers being pushed to use AI tools at all times, core teams feeling sidelined, and a major outage, arguing leadership has recast software engineering from a profit center into a disdained cost center after two decades of "move fast" success.

title: Android 17 Expands AI Agent Integration
blurb: Google released Android 17 with source now at AOSP, expanding AppFunctions so apps expose their capabilities as orchestratable tools through Android MCP, the on-device equivalent of the Model Context Protocol. AI agents like Gemini can discover and run those functions to complete workflows with direct access to an app's local state, and a new alpha Jetpack library adds them by annotating a class.

title: I Could've Rickrolled the FIFA World Cup. All I Needed Was My ID
blurb: A security researcher found that FIFA's World Cup mobile app exposed admin functions that let any verified user push content to stadium screens and ticketing systems, demonstrated by the ability to display arbitrary media during matches. The flaw stemmed from authorization checks tied only to ID verification rather than role, a common API design failure that grants legitimate users access to privileged backend operations.

title: OpenAI prepares GPT-5.6 models for the upcoming release
blurb: OpenAI is preparing to release its GPT-5.6 model family as soon as Tuesday, likely spanning standard, Mini, and Pro variants alongside a bidirectional voice model codenamed GPT-Bidi-1. Reports point to a context window near 1.5M tokens, stronger agentic coding, and deeper price cuts that would widen OpenAI's roughly 2x token-cost lead over Anthropic, whose Mythos-tier Claude Fable 5 faces US regulatory uncertainty.

title: Better Together: Amazon EKS Auto Mode and Istio Ambient Mesh
blurb: AWS walks through pairing EKS Auto Mode's automated node lifecycle with Istio Ambient Mesh's sidecar-free mTLS for large microservice fleets.

title: Introducing LifeSciBench
blurb: OpenAI released LifeSciBench, a benchmark of 750 expert-authored tasks across seven workflows and seven biological domains for testing AI on real life-science research.

title: OpenAI gets the attention it needs from AI researcher Noam Shazeer
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of "Attention Is All You Need" and a creator of the transformer architecture behind modern AI models, is leaving Google for OpenAI. Shazeer had returned to Google when it acquired his startup Character.AI for $2.7B and most recently co-led the Gemini project, making his departure a notable talent win for OpenAI ahead of a possible IPO.

title: An interview with Smartbird CEO Nadia Carlsten about the shoe company Allbirds becoming an AI infrastructure company, plans to deploy compute clusters, and more (Tim Fernholz/TechCrunch)
blurb: Allbirds, the wool-shoe maker, is pivoting into AI infrastructure under a new entity called Smartbird, with CEO Nadia Carlsten outlining plans to deploy compute clusters. The shift reflects how far capital is chasing AI buildout, pulling a consumer brand into the business of renting out GPU capacity.

title: Your old iPhone may have a security flaw Apple can’t fix
blurb: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift detailed usbliter8, a BootROM exploit affecting iPhones and Apple Watches with A12, A13, S4, and S5 chips, including the iPhone XR, XS, and 11 lines. Because the flaw sits in chip-level startup code Apple cannot patch on shipped devices, the only mitigations are avoiding untrusted USB connections or upgrading, though the attack requires physical USB access rather than remote delivery.

title: Windows 11 New Media Player Uses 3.5x More RAM, Charges for Popular Video Codecs
blurb: Windows 11's new Media Player uses roughly 377MB of RAM at idle versus about 103MB for the classic player, a 3.5x jump, and now paywalls HEVC playback behind a paid Microsoft Store extension while 24H2 drops the built-in AC-3 codec entirely. The app replaces both Groove Music and the legacy Windows Media Player by default, leaving third-party options like VLC, which bundle their own codecs, as the lighter alternative.

title: Claude Fable is relentlessly proactive
blurb: Simon Willison describes Claude Fable 5 as "relentlessly proactive," recounting how it improvised macOS screenshot tooling to debug a CSS scrollbar unprompted.

title: The ability to regrow body parts is dormant in mammals, not lost
blurb: Researchers at Texas A&M restored bone, joints, ligaments, and tendons after amputation in animals using a two-stage treatment, suggesting mammals retain a dormant regenerative capacity rather than having lost it. The method redirects the body's normal wound response away from scar-forming fibrosis and toward regrowth, and while the rebuilt tissues were imperfect, the Nature Communications study points toward reducing scarring and improving repair after amputation.

title: A 27-Year-Old Authentication Bypass in OpenBSD's PPP Stack
blurb: A zero-length PAP field made OpenBSD's bcmp credential check return 0, bypassing authentication entirely for 27 years before its 2026 fix.

title: Supermarket giant Tesco sues VMware for breach of contract (2025)
blurb: Tesco, the UK's largest supermarket chain, sued VMware for breach of contract over its software licensing and support arrangements. The dispute lands amid widespread customer backlash against Broadcom's overhaul of VMware licensing since the 2023 acquisition, which scrapped perpetual licenses for subscription bundles and left many enterprises facing steep cost increases.

title: Why Apple’s War Chest Can’t Win the Memory War
blurb: Apple’s purchasing power, long a lever for locking in cheap components, has weakened as the AI boom drives surging demand and prices for memory chips. With cloud and AI firms outbidding for the same DRAM and flash, Apple’s scale no longer guarantees favorable supply terms, squeezing margins on devices that depend on ever-larger memory configurations.

title: Norway greenlights first full-scale ship tunnel
blurb: Norway approved NOK 8.6bn (£671m) to build the Stad Ship Tunnel, the world's first full-scale ship tunnel, with construction starting in early 2027. The nearly 2km passage lets coastal ferries bypass the Stad peninsula, one of Norway's most dangerous stretches of coast where storms strike roughly 100 days a year, and is expected to open to traffic in 2031.

title: OpenAI Introduced Enterprise Usage Analytics
blurb: OpenAI introduced usage analytics and spend controls for ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins dashboards that track how teams use the product and tools to cap spending. The release targets the procurement and governance concerns that slow enterprise rollouts, shifting OpenAI's pitch from raw model capability toward the cost visibility and controls IT departments need before scaling deployments.

title: He made your free video player run smoothly. Now he’s doing that for robots.
blurb: Kyber, a Paris-based startup from VLC lead developer Jean-Baptiste Kempf, raised a $5M round led by Lightspeed to build infrastructure for controlling remote robots and drones in real time. Its core SDK synchronizes video, audio, sensor data, and control inputs with minimal latency, targeting fleets that could scale to millions of devices as physical AI shifts the bottleneck to the systems running underneath it.

title: Every fusion startup that has raised over $100M
blurb: Private fusion startups have now collectively raised billions, with Commonwealth Fusion Systems alone holding nearly $3 billion after an $863M round that closed in August. TechCrunch's roundup of every fusion company past the $100M mark reflects investor confidence built on high-temperature superconducting magnets, better AI-driven simulations, and the 2022 scientific breakeven milestone at a US Department of Energy lab.

title: Is the US government’s Anthropic ban accidentally helping the brand?
blurb: The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers reportedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's guardrails. Cybersecurity researchers signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous, and Anthropic noted the same jailbreaks exist in rival models, leaving developers building on the platform uncertain ahead of a closely watched IPO.

title: The Atlantic created a searchable database of the music used to train AI
blurb: The Atlantic published a searchable database exposing four datasets of music used to train AI models, two of which contain 12 million and 9 million tracks. Reporter Alex Reisner found Google and Stability confirmed using the sets in research papers, with songs from Lady Gaga, Radiohead, and Wu-Tang Clan downloaded via tools that bypass YouTube and Spotify terms of service.

title: SoftBank says it is struggling to find startups in Latin America ready for major investments and has completed only two new deals over the past two years (Bloomberg)
blurb: SoftBank said it is struggling to find Latin American startups ready for major investments, completing just two new deals in the region over the past two years. The slowdown marks a sharp pullback from the firm's aggressive Latin America push, when it launched dedicated funds worth billions and backed dozens of local companies during the 2021 venture boom.

title: Inside Palantir's fight to save its £330M, seven-year contract with NHS England, as public and political pressure grows to end the deal via a 2027 break clause (Financial Times)
blurb: Palantir is fighting to keep its £330M, seven-year contract to build NHS England's Federated Data Platform as opposition mounts to scrap the deal. Public and political pressure is pushing toward a 2027 break clause that would let the NHS exit early, testing the data analytics firm's ability to hold its largest UK public-sector engagement.

title: Brazil takes its National Civil Defense warning platform offline after suspected hackers send an unauthorized alert to mobile phones in several states (Mariana Catacci/CNN)
blurb: Brazil took its National Civil Defense warning platform offline after suspected hackers pushed an unauthorized alert to mobile phones across several states. The breach of an emergency-broadcast system shows how alerting infrastructure built to reach millions instantly becomes a high-value target, capable of spreading panic or eroding trust in official warnings when compromised.

title: Millions in Brazil Get Fake Government Mobile Alert After Hack
blurb: Hackers breached Brazil's civil defense alert system and pushed fake emergency notifications containing the word "misanthropy" to millions of mobile phones. The Integration and Regional Development Ministry and federal police are investigating the intrusion, which exposed how public emergency-broadcast infrastructure can be hijacked to reach an entire population at once.

title: The Memory-Chip Crisis Is Here—and You’re Footing the Bill
blurb: Memory-chip prices are climbing as AI data-center demand soaks up DRAM and NAND supply, and the increase is now reaching consumer electronics. Smartphones, game consoles, and laptops are getting more expensive as manufacturers pass higher component costs to buyers, with no relief expected until chipmakers expand capacity.

title: AMD will reinstate memory encryption on Ryzen 9000 CPUs through a BIOS update in July — TSME is coming back after 'valuable community feedback'
blurb: AMD will reinstate Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) on consumer Ryzen 9000 desktop CPUs via a BIOS update in July, reversing a quiet removal made earlier this year in the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware. The feature encrypts data in RAM to guard against cold boot attacks, and AMD said the reversal came "based on valuable community feedback" after a security researcher flagged the change on GitHub.

title: Show HN: TownSquare, a tiny presence layer for websites
blurb: TownSquare is a presence layer that adds shared cursors and live chat to any website via a single script tag, no accounts or build step.

title: The Wholesale Plagiarism of Obscure Sorrows
blurb: A cloned site at thedictionaryofobscuresorrows.com has republished the entire text of John Koenig's The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows without permission, swapping the original collage art for DALL-E 2 images and adding a GPT-4 "Submit A Sorrow" generator. Andy Newton traces how the AI-laundered copy spun up under a near-identical domain, illustrating how easily a decade-long creative project can be wholesale plagiarized and monetized.

title: Count Anything
blurb: Count Anything is a generalist text-guided object counting model spanning six visual domains, trained on the new 220K-image CLOC benchmark.

title: Ramp SWE-Bench: a private, production-grounded coding benchmark
blurb: Ramp released Ramp SWE-Bench, a private coding benchmark built from real engineering problems faced internally at the company.

title: The only scalable delete in Postgres is DROP TABLE
blurb: Postgres MVCC means large DELETEs add write and replication overhead without freeing disk; design schemas so cleanup is a DROP TABLE or TRUNCATE instead.

title: Introducing the MDN MCP server
blurb: Mozilla released an experimental MDN MCP server that pipes up-to-date web platform docs and browser compatibility data into AI coding agents.

title: Apple Prepares Second-Generation iPhone Air for Spring 2027
blurb: Apple is preparing a second-generation iPhone Air for spring 2027, aiming to boost the appeal of the ultra-thin device after a muted reception for the first model. The follow-up signals Apple is committed to the slimmed-down line rather than retiring it, betting that design refinements can win over buyers who balked at the original's tradeoffs.

title: Cursor's new model
blurb:
The context doesn't match the title. The title is "Cursor's new model," but the provided thread content is entirely about (a) an explainer on what model weights are and (b) a Perplexity Computer code-optimization thread. There's nothing in here about Cursor or any new model — no model name, no benchmarks, no release details.

I can't write an accurate blurb without fabricating the substance (model name, what it does, how it performs), which the section's whole point is to carry with specifics. Want me to:

- Write the blurb for what the context *actually* is (the model-weights explainer), under a corrected title?

title: Replit is now available in Claude
blurb: Replit is now available directly inside Claude, letting users design apps in Claude and hand them off to Replit to build and ship without copy-pasting or context switching. Claude can also delegate general development tasks to Replit through the official Replit Connector, spinning up backends or iterating on existing projects through natural language.

title: Every new iOS 27 feature that’s worth knowing about
blurb: Apple detailed the everyday-app upgrades in iOS 27 beyond its WWDC AI announcements, including Apple Intelligence-powered receipt scanning in Wallet that splits bills via Apple Cash, digitized loyalty cards, and custom-duration location sharing in Find My. Apple Maps also gains a "Local Lists" feature for discovering and saving trending spots, a move aimed at pulling users away from Google Maps, Instagram, and TikTok for local exploration.

title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has privately told ASML executives he suspects one of the company's EUV lithography machines, the only tools capable of printing the most advanced chips, may have reached China in breach of export controls. ASML denies any such system exists there, and administration officials have declined to share their evidence with either Bloomberg or the company.

title: Telegram ban in India sparks a rush to VPNs, rival apps
blurb: India temporarily blocked Telegram for a week over exam-fraud concerns, triggering the country's largest VPN download surge since at least early 2025 as users routed around the restriction. VPN downloads jumped 49% to 208,000 in a day, with Proton VPN climbing to second in Google Play's Tools category and Proton reporting Indian registrations up 120% above baseline.

title: The UK will scan asylum-seekers’ faces for age checks—despite knowing the tech is flawed
blurb: The UK will deploy AI facial age estimation next year to assess undocumented asylum seekers at its borders, the first known use of the technology for this purpose. A leaked Home Office report shows the systems regularly misclassify children as adults and perform worst on Sub-Saharan Africans, the largest group of Channel-crossing migrants, who risk being wrongly placed in adult detention.

title: A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?
blurb: NASA awarded Katalyst Space Technologies a $30M contract to build and launch a satellite that will chase down the aging Swift observatory and boost it back to a safe orbit. Swift, a $500M gamma-ray burst mission with no thrusters of its own, has decayed from 363 to 225 miles since 2004, and Katalyst plans to latch on with three robotic arms in under a year.

title: Hackers leak facial recognition records tied to millions of Madison Square Garden visitors
blurb: The cybercrime group ShinyHunters published a cache of data allegedly stolen from Madison Square Garden Entertainment after the company missed a ransom deadline. The leak reportedly includes facial recognition records, biometric tracking data, background checks, and customer correspondence tied to millions of venue visitors, turning years of controversial surveillance collection into one of the year's most sensitive breaches.

title: Open-source Vulkan driver NVK gains experimental DLSS support — bringing Nvidia’s upscaling tech to Linux via imported CUDA binaries
blurb: NVK, the community-built open-source Vulkan driver for Nvidia GPUs in Mesa, has gained experimental DLSS support in Mesa 26.2-devel, loading Nvidia's pre-compiled CUDA binaries rather than reimplementing the upscaler. The workaround sits behind an experimental flag and only works where compatible bytecode exists for a given card, closing one of the larger gaps between the open driver and Nvidia's proprietary Linux stack.

title: Sources: Virgin Media O2 and VodafoneThree have deployed tech to disable phones stolen from their stores, after phone makers resisted broader antitheft measures (Kieran Smith/Financial Times)
blurb: Virgin Media O2 and VodafoneThree have deployed technology to remotely disable phones stolen from their UK stores, according to the Financial Times. The carriers acted after handset makers resisted broader antitheft measures, putting the onus on operators to curb a surge in store thefts rather than waiting for device-level protections.

title: Sources: John Ternus gets ready to put his firm imprint on Apple's industrial design team, which has lost a true seat at Apple's exec table over the past decade (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple hardware chief John Ternus is preparing to assert tighter control over the company's industrial design team, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The group has lost influence over the past decade since Jony Ive's departure, no longer holding a dedicated seat at Apple's executive table.

title: Claude AI is better at raising funds for humans than humans, finds worrying experiment
blurb: A preprint study from Oxford and other institutions found that Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 outperformed professional human fundraisers, proving nearly three times as effective at convincing people to donate and securing gifts 13% larger across more than 1,000 conversations. The edge largely came from verbosity rather than superior reasoning, and researchers cautioned that persuasiveness did not track with accuracy, with some models producing convincing but fabricated claims.

title: AltStore PAL now available in Brazil as Apple flips the switch on alternative marketplaces [U]
blurb: Apple enabled alternative app marketplaces in Brazil, letting users install AltStore PAL and set it as their default storefront under iOS Settings. The change follows a settlement with competition watchdog CADE that also opened iOS to alternative payment systems, making Brazil the third region after Japan and the EU to allow third-party app distribution.

title: New web app can make Valve's Steam Controller drift across your desk like an RC car — web app drives the gamepad using its rumble motors
blurb: A developer's WebHID web app drives Valve's $99 Steam Controller across a desk by pulsing its rumble motors, no install required.

title: Anthropic, Amazon, and the Fable shutdown; AI-powered school arrives; World Cup tech
blurb: GeekWire Podcast on Anthropic taking its top models offline after a US order, agentic AI at Amazon, and an AI-driven Seattle school.

title: NVIDIA joins $54M bet on startup that gets AI data centres online three years faster
blurb: Verse raised $54M in a Series B led by Bessemer Venture Partners, with NVIDIA also investing, to deploy on-site battery storage that lets AI data centers skip multi-year grid interconnection queues. The approach can bring sites online up to three years sooner without cutting compute, addressing waits that now run five to seven years at major hubs.

title: Show HN: Make PDFs look scanned (CLI or in the browser via WASM)
blurb: CLI and WASM tool that degrades a PDF to look like a physical scan, with deterministic, seed-reproducible output.

title: GLM-5.2
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-source model that ranks #1 among open models and #3 globally across SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal-Bench, and NL2Repo, and can run autonomously for up to 8 hours while refining strategies over thousands of iterations. The launch lands alongside GLM-OCR, a 0.9B-parameter document model, and the GLM-4.6V vision-language family, which adds native function calling and end-to-end multimodal search-and-reasoning.

title: Robinhood launches agentic trading via MCP server for all customers
blurb: Robinhood launched agentic trading through an MCP server, letting AI assistants place and manage trades for all customers. The move plugs brokerage execution directly into the Model Context Protocol, so any MCP-compatible client can read account data and act on it, pushing automated trading from a developer experiment toward a mainstream retail feature.

title: Production Infrastructure for AI Agents
blurb: Vercel released eve, an open-source framework for building and running production AI agents, with durable execution, sandboxed compute, human-in-the-loop approvals, subagents, and evals built in. An agent is defined as a directory of files for its model, instructions, tools, skills, subagents, channels, and schedules, aiming to replace the hand-rolled plumbing agents currently require the way Next.js standardized web development.

title: Only half of planned 2026 US data center capacity is actually under construction
blurb: Only half of the data center capacity that US operators planned to bring online in 2026 is actually under construction, according to new industry tracking. The gap between announced buildouts and physical construction suggests much of the AI infrastructure boom remains on paper, raising questions about whether projected compute capacity will arrive on schedule.

title: Nuvei to buy Payoneer for $2.75B
blurb: Nuvei agreed to acquire cross-border payments platform Payoneer for $2.75B, expanding its reach into small-business and freelancer transactions across more than 190 countries. The deal pairs Nuvei's merchant payment infrastructure with Payoneer's global account network, consolidating two mid-sized players as the cross-border payments market continues to concentrate.

title: Reinforcement learning towards broadly and persistently beneficial models
blurb: OpenAI's alignment team trained a model with a small amount of "beneficial trait" data, conversations across health, law, science, and other domains designed to test honesty, corrigibility, and concern for human welfare, mixed into normal post-training. The resulting alignment gains generalized to dozens of benchmarks beyond the training domains and held up under adversarial pressure, suggesting beneficial behavior can spread the same way emergent misalignment does.

title: Secretive Wall Street Powerhouse Jane Street Seizes the AI Spotlight
blurb: Jane Street, the secretive quantitative trading firm, has emerged as a major force in AI by applying its machine-learning expertise to markets and aggressively recruiting top researchers. The firm has grown from a handful of staffers to 3,500 and plans to hire more than 500 employees this year, putting it in direct competition with frontier labs for talent.

title: SF-based Humble Robotics, which is developing an electric, self-driving cabless freight truck with 200 miles range and 55 mph max speed, raised $24M (Caroline Petrow-Cohen/Los Angeles Times)
blurb: Humble Robotics raised $24M to build an electric, self-driving freight truck that ships without a cab, designed for short-haul routes rather than long-distance highway hauling. The San Francisco startup's first vehicle tops out at 55 mph with a 200-mile range, betting that cabless design cuts cost and weight for regional logistics rather than competing with driver-replacing highway trucks.

title: Introducing Lakehouse//RT: Real-Time Performance on a Unified Lakehouse
blurb: Databricks introduced Lakehouse//RT, a real-time data warehouse that delivers millisecond query performance directly on the lakehouse without copying data into a separate serving layer. Powered by a new engine called Reyden, it targets operational analytics, BI, and app serving, with preview users reporting up to 16x faster response times than dedicated serving layers and latencies as low as 10ms on smaller datasets.

title: Claude Code now supports artifacts
blurb: Anthropic added artifacts to Claude Code, letting a session publish its work as a live, interactive web page built from the full context of the codebase, connectors, and conversation. The pages cover PR walkthroughs, incident timelines, dashboards, and release checklists, update themselves as work progresses, and share via a versioned link so teammates view the same context without status-update meetings.

title: Go eyes robotaxis and acquisitions after Japan’s biggest IPO of 2026 — here’s why it matters
blurb: Go raised $553M in Japan's biggest IPO of 2026, earmarking proceeds for robotaxi R&D and acquisitions amid a driver shortage.

title: Why the Memory Crunch Is Almost Impossible to Solve
blurb: Memory chips have become the tech industry's tightest bottleneck as AI demand outstrips production capacity and supply stays effectively fixed for years. The author argues the crunch is structurally hard to fix because building new fabs takes years, the market is concentrated among a few suppliers, and national-security limits on Chinese chips remove a relief valve, leaving consumer devices to absorb rising prices.

title: Google Is Using Nvidia’s Playbook to Build a Rival AI Chip Business
blurb: Google is leveraging its data-center scale and cash reserves to sell its in-house TPU chips to outside customers, directly challenging Nvidia's dominance in AI silicon. The company is borrowing Nvidia's own strategy of bundling chips with software and infrastructure to court data-center clients, positioning its tensor processing units as an alternative to the GPUs that power most AI training today.

title: The FBI built its own replica small town to simulate real-world cyberattacks
blurb: The FBI built a 22,000-square-foot replica town at its Huntsville, Alabama campus, complete with houses, a hospital, a power company, and traffic lights, to train investigators on simulated cyberattacks. The Kinetic Cyber Range has trained more than 1,400 students since opening in February 2025, as the FBI's latest report logged a record $20.9B in U.S. cybercrime losses, up 26% year over year.

title: Fastest, Largest, Strongest: NVIDIA Blackwell Sweeps MLPerf Training 6.0
blurb: NVIDIA's Blackwell platform swept MLPerf Training 6.0, posting the fastest time to train on all seven benchmarks and the only submissions across the full suite. The round added DeepSeek-V3 671B and GPT-OSS-20B mixture-of-experts workloads, and NVIDIA's new GB300 NVL72 racks delivered up to 1.6x faster training than GB200 NVL72 at the same scale.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the maker of the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B in its largest deal to date. The purchase pulls a leading AI-assisted development platform into Elon Musk's orbit, expanding SpaceX beyond aerospace and intensifying competition with GitHub Copilot and other coding-agent tools.

title: Data Processing is Becoming a GPU Workload
blurb: High-value data processing is shifting from CPU-based SQL ETL to GPU inference over unstructured, multimodal data like video, audio, and PDFs.

title: Apple delves deeper into payments
blurb: Apple is expanding its payments push with iOS 27, adding a "tap to share" feature that sends billing, shipping, and loyalty details to merchants, plus an enhanced Wallet showing loyalty balances, debit funds, buy-now-pay-later options, and digital IDs. The features arrive later in 2026 across roughly 2.5 billion active devices, alongside an AI bill-splitting tool and a revamped Siri powered by Google's Gemini.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
blurb: Stripe and AWS launched tools that let AI agents pay content owners and publishers for access to their material. The integration lets sites charge agents per request or per piece of content rather than blocking them, giving publishers a metering and monetization layer as automated crawlers and shopping agents become a larger share of web traffic.

title: Kodak Admits Data Breach After ShinyHunters Hack Claims
blurb: Kodak confirmed a data breach after ShinyHunters claimed to steal 2.2M customer records and threatened to leak them absent a ransom.

title: AI data centers just got a government-mandated fast lane to the grid
blurb: FERC ordered six major grid operators to fast-track interconnection requests from data centers and other large power users, with the centers paying their own connection costs. The unanimous orders also push operators to consider alternative transmission tech like solid-state transformers, but they sidestep the underlying generating-capacity shortage as data center electricity demand is projected to nearly triple through 2035.

title: Apple’s design studio has lost nearly every Jony Ive-era designer. Incoming CEO John Ternus says he’ll fix it.
blurb: Apple's industrial design studio, once the most influential product design operation in tech, has been hollowed out since Jony Ive left in 2019, with nearly every Ive-era designer having departed for LoveFrom, new ventures, or retirement. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman reports that incoming CEO John Ternus, who takes over from Tim Cook on September 1, is preparing a major shake-up to rebuild the team's eroded influence before he starts.

title: Etzioni on AI: What the World Cup tells us about the best roles for humans and machines
blurb: The 2026 World Cup has wired AI into officiating, with Sony's Hawk-Eye running video review, goal-line decisions, and semi-automated offside through 16 calibrated cameras and a sensor inside the ball that recently reversed a wrongly disallowed Sweden goal. Etzioni argues the case illustrates the best division of labor: narrow neural networks tuned for one job generate the read, while a human still signs off on the call.

title: Nearly 60% of TikTok videos shown to new users are AI slop, study finds
blurb: A Kapwing study found 59% of TikTok videos shown to new accounts are AI slop, with kids' content worst-hit.

title: Ingenious modder converts countertop ice machine into an RTX 3060 GPU cooler with a beer fridge thermostat — mod reduces temps by up to 62% in games, Cyberpunk 2077 runs at 22°C
blurb: A YouTuber converted a countertop ice machine into an RTX 3060 liquid cooler, dropping in-game temps by up to 62%.

title: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after blockbuster IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B in all-stock, days after Cursor's blockbuster IPO, to shore up its struggling AI division. The pitch to IPO investors leaned on a claimed $26 trillion addressable market in AI, a number that has to absorb a frontier coding tool bolted onto a rocket company. The deal signals that the largest private hardware players now see AI tooling as core infrastructure worth paying public-market premiums to own outright, even when the strategic fit is far from obvious.

title: The Transfer Market
blurb: Wise has built a $19M-customer business serving not one audience but a patchwork of niches: expats paying foreign mortgages, workers supporting family abroad, small businesses moving money, and now travelling football fans buying World Cup dollars. Roughly 70% of growth comes through word-of-mouth, and the company is now spending upwards of 5% of revenue on marketing to scale beyond it. The hard part is branding one product that means something different to every fragment.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney, best known for AI image generation, announced its first hardware product and a move into personal health and medical devices, starting with an ultrasound machine shown at a San Francisco event. The pivot pushes a software-only company into regulated medical hardware, a market with FDA clearance timelines and manufacturing costs that have little in common with shipping a generative model. It is a reminder that the AI labs with strong brands and cash are willing to leave their original category entirely when they see a bigger opening.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, days after the biggest IPO ever, sending its shares up 16% to make it the fourth most valuable US company. The deal folds Cursor into xAI to take on Anthropic and OpenAI in coding, but Cursor's market share has already slid from 41% to 26% in the past year while Anthropic now controls half the category. SpaceX is paying a premium for momentum that is fading, betting frontier AI distribution matters more than the share it bought.

title: Companies rein in AI usage as costs strain budgets
blurb: Companies that rushed AI into everything are now pulling it back as the bills come due, with usage-based pricing turning every query into a line item that finance can't ignore. The pattern is the same across teams: frontier models wired into workflows that don't need them, costs scaling with adoption instead of value. The strain is forcing a reckoning on where AI actually earns its spend, and the budgets are tightening faster than the deployments can prove themselves.

title: Lithuanian startup launches open-source network to detect Shahed-type drones
blurb: Lithuanian startup Mainline launched Drone Radar, an open-source network that turns volunteers' spare Android phones into acoustic sensors that listen for the low-frequency engine signatures of Shahed-type drones and triangulate their location when several nearby devices catch the same signal. The team of 20 is aiming for 10,000 active participants across the Baltics and Poland, with plans to add audio from home surveillance cameras and sensors on mobile operators' towers. It points to a wider pattern of crowdsourced, low-cost defense infrastructure built from consumer hardware rather than procurement contracts.

title: From $5B to $13B in five months: Baseten reportedly closing in on $1.5B raise
blurb: Baseten is reportedly closing in on a $1.5B round at up to a $13B valuation, less than three times its $5B mark from five months ago and roughly six times its valuation a year ago. The capital is chasing inference, not models, with the company citing estimates that inference will hit two-thirds of all AI compute by the end of 2026, up from a third in 2023. The deal is split-priced, some investors entering at an $11B floor and others paying the full $13B headline, a structure late-stage AI companies are using to raise large sums fast.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: Ahrefs checked server logs across 137K domains and found that 97% of published llms.txt files got zero traffic in May 2026, and 96% of the requests that did land came from bots, not AI assistants. The named AI tools that bother fetching them are coding agents like GPTBot and Claude-Code parsing developer docs, while AI search engines ignore the file entirely and never go looking for ones that don't exist. Unless you sell to developers reading your documentation, the file is buying you nothing, and your time is better spent on content the crawlers actually read.

title: Hyundai buys Boston Dynamics
blurb: Hyundai is buying SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325 million, giving the automaker full ownership of one of the few humanoid robotics companies with real factory work in sight. The strategic edge is that Hyundai doesn't have to imagine its first customer: it owns the plants, the vehicle programs, and now the robots, with a production Atlas slated to start parts sequencing at its Georgia EV plant by 2028. The bar is steep, with CEO Robert Playter saying Atlas needs to learn new tasks in a day or two and hit 99.9% reliability before it earns a place on the floor.

title: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of the original Transformer paper and one of the most influential researchers in modern AI, is leaving Google to join OpenAI. He called it a difficult decision after years building with the team at Google, where his work underpinned much of the field's current architecture. The move signals that the talent war at the frontier is still being fought one senior researcher at a time, and the labs that win it are the ones willing to pry loose the people who defined the last decade of progress.

title: Google DeepMind Explores the Path to ASI
blurb: Google DeepMind released a report mapping how AI might keep advancing past human-level AGI toward artificial superintelligence, a system more capable than entire organizations of people. It lays out four routes to get there — scaling AGI, paradigm shifts, recursive self-improvement, and intelligence emerging from large multi-agent collectives — and the frictions that could stall each one. The takeaway for founders is that AGI may not be a single step change to plan around, but a long run of compounding breakthroughs across science and technology, with the timing deeply uncertain.

title: An interview with Smartbird CEO Nadia Carlsten about the shoe company Allbirds becoming an AI infrastructure company, plans to deploy compute clusters, and more (Tim Fernholz/TechCrunch)
blurb: Allbirds, the sustainable shoe company, is pivoting into AI infrastructure under the Smartbird banner, with CEO Nadia Carlsten laying out plans to deploy compute clusters. It is the latest sign that the AI buildout is pulling capital and reinvention from corners of the market that have nothing to do with software, as a consumer brand bets its future on selling compute instead of footwear. The move tracks a broader pattern of struggling public companies repositioning around AI to reset their story and their valuation.

title: Every fusion startup that has raised over $100M
