Organizations running affected GlobalProtect portals should apply the vendor's fix immediately and hunt for signs of exploitation, treating exposed appliances as potential footholds for attackers.

title: I found 10k GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware
blurb: A researcher uncovered roughly 10,000 GitHub repositories distributing Trojan malware, each cloned from a legitimate project with its commits and contributors copied to appear authentic. The repos add a zip archive link to the readme containing a loader executable and a malicious lua51.dll, re-pushing the same commit every few hours to evade takedown; defenders should treat GitHub readme-hosted archive links as untrusted and block the pattern.

title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
blurb: Federal prosecutors charged 21-year-old Anthony Belford with cyberstalking after he allegedly ran a months-long harassment campaign against a former classmate, using spoofed Instagram, LinkedIn, Reddit, X, Strava, and Yahoo accounts to distribute AI-generated nude images and fabricated racist messages. Federal law bars sharing non-consensual intimate images, including synthetic ones, and the DOJ urged victims to report violations to the FBI and flag unremoved content to the FTC.

title: The UK will scan asylum-seekers’ faces for age checks—despite knowing the tech is flawed
blurb: The UK Home Office will deploy facial age estimation to assess undocumented asylum seekers at the border starting next year, despite an internal report showing the systems regularly misclassify children as adults. A WIRED and Lighthouse Reports investigation found the best-performing of seven tested algorithms degraded significantly on Sub-Saharan Africans, the largest group subject to age assessments in 2025.

title: Your browser tab could become encrypted storage for someone else’s files
blurb: A research paper from IENYC's Gregory Magarshak describes Safecloud, a decentralized storage design where browser tabs act as encrypted storage nodes: files are chunked and encrypted on the owner's device before leaving, so storage nodes ("Drops") hold only ciphertext in IndexedDB and routing nodes ("Jets") never see keys. Every key derives from a single 32-byte root secret, and three overlapping trees handle integrity, confidentiality, and authorization over one addressing path, enabling content-addressed dedup and single-key segment streaming without exposing plaintext to any participant.

title: In Other News: Apple Patches Beats Eavesdropping Flaw, DOT Closes Delta CrowdStrike Probe, AWS Continuum
blurb: Apple patched a Beats eavesdropping flaw, the DOT closed its Delta-CrowdStrike outage probe, and AWS previewed Continuum for vulnerability prioritization.

title: CISA gives federal agencies as little as three days to patch the riskiest bugs
blurb: CISA issued a new directive requiring federal civilian agencies to remediate the highest-risk vulnerabilities in as little as three days, replacing the static deadlines of the prior Known Exploited Vulnerabilities process with risk-based prioritization. Agencies must now triage flaws by exploitability and exposure rather than a uniform timeline, compressing patch windows for internet-facing systems most likely to be targeted.

title: How Dropbox uses MCP and Dash to close the design-to-code security gap
blurb: Dropbox built a system that combines MCP, foundational LLMs, and its Dash content index to automatically pull the relevant threat model during code review and check whether code changes meet the security requirements agreed on during design. Because Dash already indexes years of security reviews and engineering docs, the system closes the design-to-code gap without engineers manually linking threat models to pull requests. The post covers the architecture and what months of threat-model analysis revealed.

title: SPHINCS-: Efficient Stateless Post-Quantum Signature Verification on the EVM
blurb: A Solidity SPHINCS+ variant verifies NIST-aligned post-quantum signatures on Ethereum at ~150K gas, no precompile or protocol change required.

title: Apple adds keylogger to iOS App Store for targeted advertising: tied to your account and unencrypted
blurb: Researchers found the iOS App Store logs every tap and keystroke unencrypted, tied to your account, with no opt-out.

title: Ivanti Sentry flaw is actively exploited in the wild
blurb: CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity (CVSS 10) OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry, is being actively exploited, with Shadowserver reporting that exposed gateways were backdoored within a day of patch release and that unpatched, internet-facing instances are likely already compromised. CISA added it to the KEV catalog and gave federal agencies three days to patch under BOD 26-04; defenders should restrict admin port 8443, patch immediately, and hunt for backdoors on any exposed appliance.

title: How attackers are jailbreaking LLMs with CTF framing and how to catch them
blurb: Sysdig's Threat Research Team documented a "jailbreak-to-deploy" pattern where attackers frame exploit requests to their own upstream LLMs as CTF challenges or CVE-hunting exercises, coaxing the models past safety training into writing working exploit code they ship nearly verbatim. The framing leaks into telemetry: CVE-templated strings like ctf-litellm-cve42271-mcp-stdio/1.0 appeared in User-Agents, password fields, and AWS roleSessionNames across campaigns hitting PraisonAI, LiteLLM, FastGPT, Open-WebUI, and Gotenberg. Hunt for those baked-in CTF/CVE labels as detection artifacts.

title: Identity verification on Claude
blurb: Anthropic is rolling out identity verification on Claude, requiring a government-issued photo ID and a live selfie through verification partner Persona for certain capabilities, platform-integrity checks, and compliance measures. Persona, not Anthropic, collects and stores the ID and selfie data, with Anthropic acting as data controller and accessing records only when needed, such as reviewing an appeal.

title: Microsoft’s June update fixed 208 security flaws and introduced a cascade of new bugs across all Windows versions
blurb: Microsoft's June Patch Tuesday update KB5094126 patched a record 208 vulnerabilities on June 9 but introduced regressions across every supported Windows version, including Recycle Bin display glitches, BitLocker recovery-screen lockouts on enterprise hardware, OneDrive failures on domain-joined PCs, and system freezes. Microsoft traced the Recycle Bin bug to a hardening fix for a 23-year-old Windows Shell buffer flaw and won't ship a correction until July 14.

title: Hackers leak facial recognition records tied to millions of Madison Square Garden visitors
blurb: ShinyHunters published a stolen data cache from Madison Square Garden Entertainment after the company missed a ransom deadline, exposing facial recognition and biometric tracking records, background-check data, internal security assessments, and customer correspondence tied to millions of venue visitors. Unlike credential dumps, the leaked biometric identifiers cannot be rotated, so organizations running facial recognition at the edge should treat their collected datasets as high-value extortion targets and minimize biometric retention accordingly.

title: AryStinger botnet infected thousands of D-Link routers worldwide
blurb: AryStinger, a previously undocumented botnet detailed by Qianxin's XLab, has compromised more than 4,000 outdated D-Link DIR-850L and DIR-818LW routers by chaining old flaws including CVE-2013-3307, CVE-2016-5681, and CVE-2025-11837. Infected devices become remote "executors" for distributed scanning, proxying, tunneling, and command execution, and can also hijack DNS and sniff traffic, so operators of affected models should patch or retire them and rotate any credentials exposed through the device.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA urged Fortinet customers to secure their devices after FortiBleed exposed plaintext VPN and firewall credentials for roughly 74,000 FortiGate appliances across 194 countries. Affected organizations including Samsung, Mercedes-Benz, Chevron, and AT&T should terminate SSL VPN and admin sessions, reset passwords, and enable phishing-resistant MFA, as threat actors are already using the leaked credentials against internet-facing devices.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift released usbliter8, an unpatchable exploit that achieves code execution inside the SecureROM boot chain of Apple's A12 and A13 chips, covering the iPhone XS through 11 lines, the 2nd-gen iPhone SE, several iPads, and Apple Watch Series 4 and 5. The flaw is burned into silicon and requires physical DFU-mode access over USB, so the exposure for enterprise fleets is lost or stolen older devices rather than remote compromise.

title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
blurb: Cisco-owned Splunk confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in its PostgreSQL sidecar that lets remote attackers create or truncate arbitrary files and, per WatchTowr's published PoC, achieve remote code execution. CISA added it to the KEV catalog on June 18 with a June 21 federal deadline, so IT teams running Splunk Enterprise 10.0 or 10.2 should patch to the fixed releases now.

title: Microsoft links Mastra AI supply chain attack to North Korean hackers
blurb: Microsoft attributed the Mastra AI supply chain attack, which compromised more than 140 npm packages in the @mastra scope, to North Korean state actor Sapphire Sleet after attackers hijacked a maintainer account to inject a dayjs typosquat that dropped a cross-platform credential and crypto-wallet stealer. The campaign reinforces npm dependency provenance as an enterprise procurement concern, pushing IT teams toward maintainer-account hardening, install-hook controls, and software supply chain monitoring across developer endpoints.

title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
blurb: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation is shipping GentleKiller, an EDR-killer framework spanning eight variants that hunts 400 processes tied to 48 security products before deploying its encryptor, abusing vulnerable drivers from Kaspersky, FACEIT, and Valorant. The group has claimed 504 victims since March 2025 and operationalizes newly disclosed BYOVD exploits within days, raising the bar for endpoint defenses across enterprise security stacks.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards integration on June 11 after attackers stole OAuth tokens from Klue's compromised integration infrastructure and used them to reach customer Salesforce data, including records belonging to Huntress. The breach, attributed to the Icarus extortion group, hit a third-party app connection rather than the Salesforce platform itself, putting the spotlight on OAuth token governance for connected enterprise apps.

title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Huntress and Recorded Future disclosed they were hit by a supply chain attack on market intelligence platform Klue, which began June 11 when attackers harvested OAuth tokens and abused the Salesforce REST API to exfiltrate CRM data, including business contacts and contract details. The incident was confined to the Klue-Salesforce integration and follows the same playbook as recent Salesloft Drift and Gainsight breaches, signaling that third-party SaaS integrations remain a soft entry point into enterprise Salesforce instances.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: A law-enforcement operation across the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, backed by Europol, took down 106 command-and-control servers and cleaned malware from 14,971 infected WordPress sites running the SocGholish loader. Active since 2017 and tied to the Evil Corp-linked broker TA569, SocGholish exposed roughly 55% of cloud customers this year, so IT teams should force credential resets and MFA on any compromised CMS accounts.

title: DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic
blurb: DragonForce ransomware affiliates deployed a custom Go-based RAT, Backdoor.Turn, that tunnels command-and-control traffic through Microsoft's legitimate Teams TURN relay infrastructure, leaving defenders to see only outbound connections to trusted Microsoft servers. The technique, documented by Symantec and Carbon Black after a one-to-two-month intrusion at a major US services firm, defeats network monitoring that allowlists Microsoft domains and pressures IT teams to inspect Teams relay traffic they previously trusted.

title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
blurb: Klue confirmed attackers stole OAuth tokens from a compromised legacy integration credential on June 12, then used them to exfiltrate Salesforce CRM data from multiple customer environments, including Huntress, with the Icarus extortion group now claiming responsibility. The breach extends the pattern of OAuth-token abuse against third-party SaaS integrations, putting pressure on enterprise buyers to audit every connected app with standing Salesforce access and revoke unused tokens.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco is migrating off VMware across its fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known exit from the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition, after facing a 175% price hike and a 350% upcharge on mainframe software. The move follows Tesco's $134M lawsuit alleging Broadcom breached perpetual licenses bought in 2021, and signals that Broadcom's subscription pivot is pushing even its largest enterprise accounts to rip and replace.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, harvesting verified working credentials rather than exploiting any Fortinet flaw, according to SOCRadar. Telecommunications, government, and critical-infrastructure organizations dominate the victim list, and over 20% of affected devices belong to enterprises with more than $1B in annual revenue, putting weak default and long-lived admin accounts squarely in scope for credential audits.

title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
blurb: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach at its third-party license-system vendor that exposed driver's license details, passport numbers, and contact information for 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers, though SSNs and financial data were not affected. The Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion, and TPWD is offering impacted individuals one year of free credit monitoring while working with the vendor to add new safeguards.

title: Google workspace threatening to block Firefox access
blurb: Google Workspace began showing Firefox users an interstitial urging them to switch to Chrome to access admin.google.com, which Google support framed as a non-blocking security recommendation it would not document publicly. The move revives concerns that Google leverages Workspace's enterprise footprint to steer admins toward Chrome, and it gives IT teams standardized on Firefox a new reason to scrutinize browser lock-in across their Google stack.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco agreed to acquire identity lifecycle security firm WideField Security to fold human and non-human identity discovery, session monitoring, and behavioral analytics into Splunk's Agentic SOC and Cisco's data fabric. The deal, Cisco's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix, adds credential and session context to threat investigations, positioning the combined stack to govern autonomous AI activity for enterprises standardized on Splunk.

title: 칼럼 | 미토스가 바꾼 것은 공격 방식이 아니다, 대응 시간이다
blurb: The author argues that Anthropic's Mythos model, which found 2,000+ unknown vulnerabilities and wrote working exploits unprompted in seven weeks, doesn't introduce a new threat class but collapses the response window that patching and threat-sharing consortiums depend on. Over 99% of those flaws remain unpatched, and Anthropic's Glasswing early-access program for Microsoft, AWS, JPMorgan, and 50-odd partners splits security into two tiers. Mid-market firms outside it should assume compromise and harden identity infrastructure now rather than wait for guidance.

title: New attack turned Microsoft 365 Copilot into 1-click data theft tool
blurb: Microsoft patched a critical Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise vulnerability, dubbed SearchLeak and rated CVE-2026-42824 at maximum severity, that let a single malicious link exfiltrate data from a victim's mailbox, OneDrive, and SharePoint. Varonis researchers chained a prompt injection, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing SSRF-enabled CSP bypass so a victim's click alone triggered Copilot to search company data and leak it to an attacker server.

title: New Prinz Eugen ransomware prioritizes recent files for encryption
blurb: New Go-based Prinz Eugen ransomware encrypts recently modified files first, skips RaaS, and uses RDP credentials plus legitimate RMM tools for access.

title: Anthropic launches managed connector access with Okta
blurb: Anthropic launched Enterprise-Managed Authorization for MCP connectors, letting admins provision connector access across Claude chat, Claude Code, and Cowork through their identity provider, starting with Okta. Built on an open MCP extension, the feature ties permissions and revocation to IdP group assignments for vendors including Asana, Atlassian, Figma, Linear, and Supabase, with Slack support planned and HubSpot, Ramp, and Webflow already deploying it.

title: US energy regulator to order grid operators to expedite AI data center applications — says projects should bring their own power or cut usage during high demand
blurb: The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission is preparing an order requiring grid operators to fast-track AI data center interconnections, prioritizing projects that bring their own power or curtail demand during peak stress, with feasibility studies due in 90 days. The move aligns with Trump's AI Action Plan but lands as grid operators pass buildout costs to ratepayers, including PJM Interconnection's 75.5% power-cost hike and a $2B infrastructure charge Maryland is contesting before FERC.

title: Why Use App-Level Auth When Every Database Has Auth? (Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 Pre-Auth RCE)
blurb: watchTowr published a technical analysis of CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution flaw in Splunk Enterprise's PostgreSQL Sidecar Service Endpoint that carries a 9.8 CVSS score. The PostgreSQL sidecar is not installed or enabled in every deployment, so exposure depends on configuration, but SIEM operators running affected versions should apply Splunk's June 10th advisory and audit whether the endpoint is reachable.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: AWS is in early talks to sell its homegrown Trainium AI chips to third parties for use in their own data centers, AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg, a move CEO Andy Jassy pegged at a potential $50B annual run rate. The shift would pit Amazon directly against Nvidia's $326B run rate, but AWS would have to divert silicon from sold-out internal capacity and secure TSMC supply already dominated by Nvidia.

title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
blurb: Salesforce suspended its integration with Klue's Battlecards app on June 17 after detecting unauthorized access to customer data via the app's OAuth connection, making it the third compromised third-party integration in the ongoing campaign that hit Salesloft Drift and Gainsight. Attackers used compromised Klue OAuth tokens to exfiltrate data through the Salesforce REST API within 24 hours, with cybersecurity vendor Huntress among the confirmed victims.

title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
blurb: A data leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed Fortinet and FortiGate SSL VPN credentials, including plaintext passwords, for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations including Chevron, Samsung, AT&T, and Mercedes-Benz. Researcher Bob Diachenko tied the cache to a Russian-speaking group that ran roughly 1.16 billion credential attempts against FortiGate targets, making credential rotation and SSL VPN review an immediate priority for affected enterprises.

title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 26H2, urges IT admins to prepare for release
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 26H2 and told IT admins to begin prepping rollout, shipping it as a small enablement package that toggles features already present in the shared servicing branch rather than a full OS replacement. The eKB approach means faster, lower-disruption deployment for 24H2 and 25H2 fleets, with 24-month consumer and 36-month Enterprise support cycles and an expected early-October 2026 rollout.

title: Accenture to buy Dragos, runZero, and NetRise in $4.2 billion cybersecurity deal
blurb: Accenture agreed to buy a majority stake in OT-security vendor Dragos plus all of runZero and NetRise for $4.2B, combining threat detection, attack-surface intelligence, and firmware-level visibility into one operational-technology security platform for critical infrastructure operators. The deal pushes Accenture deeper into the under-funded "xOT" market spanning power grids, pipelines, and manufacturing, positioning it against pure-play OT security firms as buyers consolidate IT and OT defense procurement.

title: Solving an ARD problem in AI: Agentic Resource Discovery
blurb: Google, Microsoft, Cisco, Nvidia, Salesforce and others proposed Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD), an open protocol that lets AI agents find and safely invoke the tools and services available inside a corporate domain. ARD adds a missing layer over siloed registries through published capability catalogs and crawling registries, giving enterprises a standard way to govern which tools agents reach without wiring every system together by hand.

title: AWS aims to take the pain out of RAG with Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base
blurb: AWS released Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base, a managed RAG service that automates embeddings, indexing, and retrieval tuning while shipping six native connectors for S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Google Drive, OneDrive, and web content. The service picks and maintains default embedding, re-ranker, and foundation models on the customer's behalf, letting enterprise teams skip the vector-database and pipeline upkeep that often outweighs the AI application itself.

title: 미국 AI 수출 규제에 막힌 앤트로픽, 신분증 인증으로 돌파구 찾나
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to block non-citizens from its newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, on national security grounds, but Anthropic disabled both for all customers because it cannot currently distinguish foreign nationals from US citizens. A privacy policy update effective July 8 lets Anthropic collect government-issued ID to verify citizenship, giving consumers a workaround via US passports or enhanced driver's licenses, while enterprise customers, the heaviest users of the new models, wait for another fix.

title: Microsoft will soon allow some users to block Copilot from analyzing their Office files
blurb: Microsoft is updating a Purview sensitivity-label setting to block Copilot and other connected AI services from analyzing labeled Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files, enforcing the restriction across all connected experiences rather than exempting AI tools. The change, tagged MC1297982, applies only to commercial customers with a Purview license and rolls out in phases through the end of next month, with already-labeled files upgraded automatically.

title: Google Hits 50% IPv6
blurb: Google's measurement data shows IPv6 traffic to its services crossing 50% of users for the first time, a milestone that signals the protocol is mature and deployed at global scale. APNIC Labs puts worldwide IPv6 capability lower at 42% due to different sampling and statistical weighting, and adoption remains uneven across economies, so enterprises running dual-stack networks still cannot assume IPv6 reach in any given market.

title: Supermarket giant Tesco sues VMware for breach of contract (2025)
blurb: Tesco filed a breach-of-contract suit against VMware in the UK High Court, escalating enterprise pushback against Broadcom's post-acquisition licensing changes to perpetual licenses and support terms. The case signals that large VMware customers are now willing to litigate rather than absorb the subscription-bundle repricing Broadcom imposed, raising the stakes for IT leaders weighing renewal terms against migration to Nutanix, Proxmox, or hyperscaler alternatives.

title: Windows 11 New Media Player Uses 3.5x More RAM, Charges for Popular Video Codecs
blurb: Microsoft's new Media Player, now the default across Windows 11 in place of Groove Music and the classic player, idles at 377MB of RAM versus roughly 103MB for the legacy app and paywalls HEVC playback behind a paid Store extension while 24H2 drops the built-in AC-3 codec. IT teams standardizing Windows 11 images face higher per-endpoint memory overhead and added codec licensing friction, pushing some toward third-party players like VLC.

title: Why Amazon hates 'human-in-the-loop' AI governance
blurb: Amazon Security VP Eric Brandwine argues that human-in-the-loop is not the gold standard for governing agentic AI, because humans are non-deterministic, inconsistent, and prone to the same mistakes and confabulation as LLMs. His sharper point is normalization of deviance: human reviewers tune out repeated false alarms and stop responding, so a rubber-stamping human in the loop offers enterprises less real oversight than the control its presence implies.

title: The Memory-Chip Crisis Is Here—and You’re Footing the Bill
blurb: Memory prices are climbing across smartphones, laptops, game consoles, and servers as DRAM and NAND suppliers redirect capacity toward AI datacenter demand, pushing up the cost of nearly every device that ships with memory. IT buyers face higher unit prices and tighter availability on PC and server refreshes, so hardware budgets set before the shortage will need to absorb the increase or stretch refresh cycles longer.

title: OpenAI Introduced Enterprise Usage Analytics
blurb: OpenAI added usage analytics and spend controls to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins seat-level visibility into consumption and the ability to cap spending across teams. The tooling addresses a recurring procurement complaint for IT buyers managing large rollouts, where unbounded usage made costs hard to forecast and chargeback to business units nearly impossible.

title: Inside Palantir's fight to save its £330M, seven-year contract with NHS England, as public and political pressure grows to end the deal via a 2027 break clause (Financial Times)
blurb: Palantir is fighting to keep its £330M, seven-year contract to build NHS England's Federated Data Platform, as public and political pressure mounts to trigger a 2027 break clause and exit the deal. The campaign tests whether a US data-analytics vendor can hold a politically contested public-sector contract, and signals procurement risk for any supplier whose NHS work draws sustained scrutiny over patient-data handling.

title: US energy regulator moves to speed up data center projects
blurb: FERC unanimously approved orders requiring the six largest US interstate grid operators, which serve about 200 million people, to justify or revise their data center interconnection rules in order to cut the time needed to power on new facilities. The directives prioritize flexible large loads and behind-the-meter co-location, signaling to hyperscalers like Microsoft that regulatory friction on grid access is easing as AI buildout accelerates.

title: NY man charged after harassing college student with AI-generated nudes
blurb: A New York man, 21-year-old Anthony Belford, was indicted on a federal cyberstalking charge after allegedly using fake Instagram, LinkedIn, X, and Yahoo accounts to distribute AI-generated nude images of a Georgia college student and spread fabricated racist messages in her name. The case reinforces that federal law now covers AI-generated intimate imagery shared without consent, with the DOJ pointing victims to the FBI and the FTC's Take It Down platform for content removal.

title: Microsoft finally admits its default Windows 11 25H2, 24H2 action broke key legacy component
blurb: Microsoft acknowledged that its July 2025 switch to the JScript9Legacy engine, default-enabled on Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, breaks older apps and web components by no longer persisting JScript global definitions and execution context across scripts. The fix-for-security tradeoff means enterprise IT teams running legacy line-of-business apps on these builds have likely hit silent compatibility failures for close to a year before the support article confirmed the cause.

title: Windows 11 version 26H2 is now available for testing in the latest preview build
blurb: Microsoft moved the Windows 11 Experimental Channel (formerly Dev) to version 26H2 in this week's Insider preview builds 26300.8697 and 26220.8690, with no new features beyond the version bump. The release focuses on reliability fixes, including a reworked File Explorer copy dialog in dark mode, faster Start menu app updates, and a patch for HYPERVISOR_ERROR and KMODE_EXCEPTION bugchecks hitting some devices.

title: AWS announces AWS Blocks, an open-source framework for composing application backends on AWS (Preview)
blurb: AWS released AWS Blocks into public preview, an open-source TypeScript framework that lets developers build application backends with database, auth, file uploads, and background jobs running locally before deploying to production AWS with zero code changes. The framework is free beyond standard usage-based AWS charges and supports a drop-down to AWS CDK at any point, deepening AWS lock-in while lowering the infrastructure-skills barrier for enterprise development teams.

title: A modest proposal: Reformat everything to make documents more palatable to AI
blurb: The author argues that enterprises should restructure documents into AI-friendly formats rather than expecting models to wrestle with the messy layouts humans produce, floating a standardized "DocLang" as the fix. The implication for IT leaders is a familiar trade-off: cleaner machine ingestion and better RAG accuracy on one side, a fresh content-conversion mandate and yet another format standard to govern on the other.

title: SearchLeak: How We Turned M365 Copilot Into a One-Click Data Exfiltration Weapon
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that lets a single click on a microsoft.com link silently exfiltrate a victim's mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, and OneDrive content, which Microsoft patched as critical-severity CVE-2026-42824. Because the attack rides a trusted Microsoft domain and needs no plugins or extra permissions, standard anti-phishing and URL-filtering tools miss it, putting any indexed organizational data the user can access in scope.

title: Google adds Hand gesture verification for reCAPTCHA
blurb: Google added hand gesture verification to reCAPTCHA, analyzing 21 hand-knuckle landmarks from camera video that's deleted after each challenge.

title: Microsoft’s June update fixed 208 security flaws and introduced a cascade of new bugs across all Windows versions
blurb: Microsoft's June Patch Tuesday update KB5094126 fixed a record 208 security vulnerabilities on June 9 but introduced bugs across every supported Windows and Windows Server version, including garbled Recycle Bin delete dialogs, BitLocker recovery lockouts on enterprise HP and Dell hardware, and OneDrive failures on domain-joined PCs. Microsoft confirmed the issues on June 18 and does not expect the first fix until July 14, leaving IT teams to weigh delaying the patch against its security coverage.

title: AryStinger botnet infected thousands of D-Link routers worldwide
blurb: A previously undocumented botnet called AryStinger has infected more than 4,000 outdated D-Link routers, primarily DIR-850L and DIR-818LW models, exploiting flaws dating back to CVE-2013-3307 to turn devices into proxies for scanning, tunneling, and DNS hijacking. Almost half of infections sit in South Korea, and a more advanced Go-based variant is now targeting NAS systems, raising the exposure for IT teams running unpatched edge hardware.

title: Amazon S3 annotations: attach rich, queryable context directly to your objects
blurb: AWS announced S3 annotations, letting teams attach up to 1,000 named annotations per object (1 MB each, 1 GB total) in JSON, XML, YAML, or plain text, editable without rewriting the object. Annotations travel with objects through copy, replication, and cross-region transfers, and flow into S3 Metadata tables queryable from Athena, giving agentic workflows scalable context without expensive retrieval.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare's Project Glasswing team details how to build a model-agnostic vulnerability-scanning harness, arguing that generic coding agents fail because they hold one hypothesis at a time and lose state during context compaction. The architecture treats models as interchangeable, using one for discovery and a different one for validation so findings are cross-checked by distinct logic, then traces vulnerabilities across cross-repo dependencies down to a triaged queue.

title: Alice is impatient
blurb: The author argues that SREs and customers measure time differently: thanks to the inspection paradox, users experience a t-weighted latency distribution, not your mean. A service reporting a sub-1-minute MTTR or 100ms mean request can leave customers experiencing hours of recovery or seconds of wait, because long requests and outages dominate their perception. The takeaway is to track tail latency and variance, not just averages.

title: AWS WAF adds AI traffic monetization capability to help content owners charge AI bots for content access
blurb: AWS WAF added AI traffic monetization, a Bot Control capability that lets content providers and publishers price, meter, and collect payment from AI bots and agents hitting their content and APIs. Owners set a price, accept payment through third-party providers, and grant scoped access directly at the edge, turning unwanted crawler traffic into a revenue stream without standing up separate gateway infrastructure.

title: Tesco UK supermarket chain removes 40,000 servers from VMware infrastructure — mass exodus continues due to Broadcom's aggressive subscription model
blurb: Tesco is replacing VMware across its entire fleet of 40,000 servers by 2027, the largest publicly known migration off the platform since Broadcom's 2023 acquisition and a follow-on to the $134M lawsuit Tesco filed over voided perpetual licenses. The chain cites a 175% price hike on VMware and 350% on mainframe software, plus Broadcom cutting support and security updates for non-subscription customers.

title: Cell-Based Architecture for Resilient Payment Systems
blurb: American Express detailed how its core payments platform uses cell-based architecture to isolate failures into independent cells, each running its own services and data so a localized fault stays contained instead of cascading across the platform. The post walks through the design principles the team has followed since a 2018 cloud-native rebuild, showing how cell isolation shrinks blast radius while keeping latency low and letting the platform scale capacity without expanding the failure domain.

title: What job interviews taught me about Kubernetes
blurb: The author uses Kubernetes interview questions as a lens for the internals practitioners gloss over in daily operations, walking through how the scheduler, kubelet, and control loops actually behave under questioning. The piece argues that interview prep exposes the gap between running kubectl commands and understanding why a pod is stuck pending or a node goes NotReady. Useful as a self-audit for platform engineers who operate clusters without having traced the reconciliation path end to end.

title: How we run Firecracker VMs inside EC2 and start browsers in less than 1s
blurb: Browser Use rebuilt its cloud browser infrastructure on Firecracker microVMs running nested inside EC2 instances, cutting session cost from $0.06 to $0.02 per browser-hour and starting a fully isolated Chromium in under a second. The post details how they made nested virtualization fast enough to be practical, and why they abandoned Unikraft unikernels, which idled cheaply but couldn't autoscale fast enough when traffic spiked.

title: Surprising economics of load-balanced systems
blurb: Marc Brooker works through an M/M/c queuing model where c single-request servers sit behind a load balancer, with offered load scaled linearly to hold per-server utilization constant. The counterintuitive result, derived from Erlang's C formula, is that mean client latency drops toward the one-second service floor as c grows, because larger pools queue a shrinking fraction of requests. The practical takeaway for capacity planning: bigger shared pools beat many small ones at the same utilization.

title: Epoll vs. io_uring in Linux
blurb: The author walks through why Linux gives you two models for async I/O, using a student-built reverse proxy as the throughline. epoll signals when a socket is ready but still costs two syscalls per I/O event plus a context switch each, which dominates overhead at high connection counts. io_uring instead reports when I/O is done via shared submission and completion ring buffers, batching many operations into a single io_uring_enter() call and cutting syscall traffic sharply.

title: Kubernetes in the Age of AI
blurb: An O'Reilly Radar piece tracks Kubernetes's shift from container orchestrator to default AI infrastructure platform, citing CNCF survey data that 82% of container users now run it in production, up from 66% in 2023. The article argues that operationalizing AI/ML workloads on k8s remains underserved, with practitioners flagging model serving, scheduling for compute-intensive jobs, and MLOps tooling as the gaps teams hit in production.

title: Server-Side Tools Are Now Available for DigitalOcean Inference Engine
blurb: DigitalOcean launched Server-Side Tools in public preview for its Inference Engine, letting a model run web search, web fetch, Knowledge Bases retrieval, and MCP servers from inside a single inference request. Teams enable them with an existing Model Access Key, removing the separate tool infrastructure, credentials, and orchestration layer that agentic workflows usually require to operate.

title: How We Cut Semgrep's Taint Analysis Time by 75%
blurb: Semgrep's engineering team explained how they cut interfile taint-analysis time by 75% by eliminating a redundant "run taint twice" step that recomputed taint configs and reran dataflow analysis after the interfile pass already produced them. The original design duplicated work to avoid porting intrafile dataflow logic to the new cross-file engine. The writeup walks through the sources, propagators, sanitizers, and sinks model and where the wasted computation hid.

title: Google Cloud publishes a secure MCP-on-GKE guide
blurb: Google Cloud published a walkthrough for deploying a remote MCP server on GKE Autopilot using the Streamable HTTP transport, replacing per-developer local stdio servers with a centralized, horizontally scaling endpoint. Because the servers are stateless, Autopilot absorbs traffic spikes without manual node management, and a single update propagates to every connected agent and pipeline. The guide leans on the Kubernetes Gateway API and SSL certificates to force encrypted-only traffic and block unauthorized connections.

title: Vercel Connect
blurb: Vercel released Vercel Connect in public beta, replacing long-lived provider tokens stored in environment variables with runtime credential exchange that hands agents short-lived, task-scoped tokens. Teams register a connector once per provider like Slack or GitHub, attach it to projects with access controls, and let the SDK refresh tokens automatically, shrinking the blast radius when a credential leaks.

title: Why cloud native belongs at the heart of agentic AI: Lessons from building a multi-agent security platform on Kubernetes
blurb: An engineer at Orange Innovation documents building an internal real-time security-operations platform where each AI agent runs as its own Kubernetes Deployment, not an in-process module, giving every agent independent resource limits, identity, and restart policy. The stack pairs Falco with eBPF and an Isolation Forest model to pre-filter syscall events before they reach LangGraph-driven agents coordinated over A2A. Treating agents as microservices means canary rollouts, HPA, and namespace isolation apply without reinvention.

title: Running MicroVMs in Proxmox VE, the Easy Way
blurb: The author built pve-microvm, a Debian package that makes QEMU's microvm machine type a first-class managed guest in Proxmox VE, splitting the difference between LXC containers and full VMs. It ships a custom kernel, patches Proxmox's Perl internals for web UI integration, supports 21 guest OSes, and boots in under 300ms with KVM-level isolation. The author now runs Gitea, Caddy, and mini-firewalls on it as a daily driver.

title: How We Rebuilt AI Visibility Measurement From The Ground Up
blurb: NP Digital rebuilt its AI visibility audits around the SPIV framework (Segment, Persona, Intent, Variable), which grounds tracking prompts in real user data instead of generic inputs that produce unactionable results. Because model responses are probabilistic, visibility is expressed as a distribution, with a second layer of calculated metrics like Shannon entropy, Gini coefficient, and KL divergence exposing the stability and competitive dynamics behind the surface numbers. The payoff is a forcing function: it gets harder to justify tracking low-intent queries that have no connection to how the product is actually bought.

title: How AI Search Grounding Actually Works: Google vs OpenAI vs Anthropic
blurb: This analysis ran the same query ("best ai seo agency 2026") through Google, OpenAI, and Anthropic on one day and inspected the raw grounding data each returned. The defining metric is the gap between pages received and pages cited: Gemini cites almost everything it pulls (7 received, 7 cited), OpenAI receives a flood and footnotes a trickle, and Anthropic sits in between. For teams optimizing for AI citations, that means a page good enough to rank on Google can still get dropped by OpenAI's tighter filter, so GEO measurement has to be tracked per platform rather than as one funnel.

title: Agentic marketing AI startup Gradial grabs $65M in fresh funding
blurb: Gradial raised $65M in a Series C led by Insight Partners at a $675M valuation, bringing total funding to $110M over the past 16 months. Rather than building another point tool, the Seattle startup runs an orchestration layer of AI agents across Salesforce, Adobe, ServiceNow, and Databricks to author, brand-check, and publish marketing content through existing approval flows.

title: How Attention.com Turns Sales Calls Into Pipeline: The Best GTM Data You Own, and Why Most B2B Teams Throw It Away
blurb: At SaaStr AI 2026, Attention.com CEO Anis Bennaceur argued that next quarter's pipeline already sits in the sales calls you recorded this quarter and never mined. His core play: rebuild your ICP monthly off live closed-won data instead of annually, using an agent that pulls recent wins, extracts buyer firmographics and tenure from call transcripts, and enriches them in a tool like Clay. The output is a dozen narrow buyer pockets you can write real emails to, like "the frustrated veteran stuck on an incumbent," rather than one stale broad ICP.

title: Snowflake’s CMO Runs Marketing for 700 People. She Starts Her Day By Talking to Her Data, Not a Dashboard.
blurb: Snowflake CMO Denise Persson says she no longer opens a dashboard each morning, instead querying her marketing data in plain English to get the "why" behind pipeline moves and ending the sales-marketing fight over whose numbers are right. Her mandate for the year is 40-50% growth with flat or fewer resources, on the assumption AI absorbs the load instead of new headcount. The prerequisite she stresses: invest in your data estate first, because agents amplify bad data faster and at scale.

title: TikTok bans AI-generated voices in shopping livestreams
blurb: TikTok now bans AI-generated voices in shopping livestreams, requiring that hosts' verbal interactions happen in real time rather than through synthetic or pre-recorded audio. The move pressures brands and affiliate sellers that had been scaling live commerce with AI hosts, forcing a return to human presenters or compliant real-time setups for the platform's fast-growing Shop format.

title: Nearly 60% of TikTok videos shown to new users are AI slop, study finds
blurb: Kapwing's analysis of 10,742 TikTok videos found that 59% of clips served to brand-new accounts are AI slop, roughly three times YouTube's rate, with the Kids category worst-hit at 57% and the #CartoonKids hashtag running 97% AI-generated. The finding raises brand-safety stakes for any team buying TikTok placements or chasing organic reach, since default For You inventory skews toward synthetic junk before the algorithm personalizes.

title: The Mom-and-Pop SaaS era has arrived
blurb: Elena Verna argues the bigger AI shift isn't faster developers but a collapse in the cost of building software, which makes entirely new categories economically viable. As building gets cheap, the winners will be domain experts (teachers, real estate agents, accountants, coaches) shipping "Mom-and-Pop SaaS" for problems they understand deeply, rather than a small group of venture-funded engineers in a few tech hubs.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic shipped a major update to Claude Design that lets teams import design systems from GitHub repos, design files, or uploads, then builds and auto-corrects components against those rules while round-tripping to code. The release also targets the tool's heavy token consumption, repositioning a viral research preview that hit 1M users in a week from a prototyping demo into an enterprise brand-compliance layer.

title: Secret Network’s Axelar bridge drained for $4.67 million in infinite-mint exploit that went unnoticed for seven days
blurb: Secret Network's Axelar bridge was drained for $4.67 million in an infinite-mint exploit that went undetected for seven days before the team flagged it. Roughly $770,000 of the stolen funds remains in the attacker's Axelar wallet after Axelar declined Secret Network's request to freeze the address.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft identified a USB-spreading worm called Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits that has been harvesting seed phrases, private keys, and recipient addresses from the Windows clipboard since February, exfiltrating the data over Tor. The "crypto clipper" also silently swaps in attacker-controlled wallet addresses during transfers, and Microsoft urged users to disable AutoRun and block .lnk execution on removable media.

title: Ethereum's biggest 'sandwich' bot drained of $7.5 million in ironic exploit
blurb: An attacker drained over $7.5 million from jaredfromsubway.eth, Ethereum's most active sandwich bot, by exploiting its automated trading logic rather than a contract bug or phishing. Blockaid said the attacker spent weeks deploying fake tokens and liquidity pools mimicking WETH, USDC, and USDT, baiting the bot into approving malicious helper contracts that then drained funds, some routed through Tornado Cash.

title: AWS Plugs Coinbase's x402 Into CloudFront, Letting Publishers Charge AI Agents in USDC
blurb: AWS turned on AI-traffic monetization inside AWS WAF Bot Control, letting any site behind CloudFront charge AI agents per request in USDC on Base or Solana through Coinbase's x402 protocol. A matched Monetize rule returns an HTTP 402 with a price manifest, and Coinbase's x402 Facilitator verifies the onchain payment within a single request cycle, with no new accounts, invoices, or API keys.

title: CME Group to Sue CFTC Over Perpetual Futures Approval, Citing Dodd-Frank Swaps Definition
blurb: CME Group plans to sue the CFTC over its late-May approval of crypto perpetual futures, arguing the contracts meet the Dodd-Frank definition of swaps because their funding-rate mechanism involves two parties exchanging periodic payments. A swap designation would impose a five-day margin period of risk versus one day for futures, more than doubling collateral requirements and likely pushing perpetuals offshore, as the CFTC's spokesperson called the planned suit "frivolous.

title: CLARITY Act Reaches Senate Floor With House Ready to Move Fast; Seven-Democrat Math Becomes the Gate
blurb: The Digital Asset Market Clarity Act sits on the Senate calendar eligible for a floor vote, with House Agriculture's Dusty Johnson pledging a fast companion if the Senate clears it before the August recess. Passage hinges on cloture math: Republicans' ~53 seats leave the bill roughly seven Democratic votes short, with only Gallego and Alsobrooks on record from committee and both flagging their support as contingent.

title: Congress Strikes Housing-Bill Deal That Bans Fed CBDC Through 2030
blurb: Congressional negotiators folded a statutory ban on a Federal Reserve retail CBDC into the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act (H.R. 6644), barring any Fed-issued digital dollar through December 31, 2030. The text carves out private, dollar-denominated stablecoins, slotting alongside the GENIUS Act framework and leaving issuers on payment rails to compete for years with no Fed product in the same lane.

title: How STRC lost its par: The timeline behind Strategy's preferred-stock meltdown
blurb: Strategy's preferred stock STRC, engineered to hold its $100 par value, fell to a low of $83 on Thursday, roughly 17% below target and its weakest level since debuting in July 2025. The slide followed a $1.5B convertible-note buyback at an 8% discount, falling bitcoin prices, thinner liquidity buffers, and competitive pressure from Strive's SATA, which now pays a 13% yield on a daily schedule.

title: Charles Schwab is working with Cboe to launch S&P 500 ‘yes-or-no’ binary options contracts: WSJ
blurb: Charles Schwab is working with Cboe to launch S&P 500 binary options, yes-or-no contracts that pay out based on whether the index closes above a set level, according to the WSJ. The move marks the brokerage's first push into prediction-market-style products, putting a regulated incumbent into a sector currently dominated by Kalshi and Polymarket.

title: Sonic Labs Founders Including Andre Cronje Quit Board as New CEO Pledges to Get '1% Better' Daily
blurb: Sonic Labs' three founding board members — Andre Cronje, Michael Kong and David Richardson — resigned, with Matt Visser named CEO and Kosta Kourkoumelis named COO at the network formerly known as Fantom. The shakeup follows a roughly 97% drop in the S token from its January 2025 high and a collapse in total value locked to about $20M, down 98% from a $1.14B peak.

title: TD Cowen says CME has the upper hand in lawsuit against CFTC over crypto perpetual futures
blurb: CME Group sued the CFTC over its approval of perpetual futures for Kalshi and Coinbase, and TD Cowen says CME holds the stronger case on both procedural and substantive grounds. CME argues perps should be regulated as swaps rather than futures because they never expire, and TD Cowen expects it to seek a preliminary injunction blocking US perps while the case proceeds.

title: Schwab to join prediction markets race with S&P 500 event-based options: WSJ
blurb: Charles Schwab is partnering with Cboe Global Markets to launch binary options on the S&P 500, paying a fixed amount or nothing based on whether the index closes above a preset level, in the brokerage's first move into prediction markets. The product, expected within months, puts Schwab against Kalshi, Polymarket, Coinbase, and Robinhood, though it limits contracts to financial benchmarks and avoids politics and sports.

title: Live markets: Bitcoin has traded below its mining cost for five months, squeezing miners
blurb: Bitcoin has traded below its ~$78,000 production cost for five straight months, JPMorgan said, pushing roughly 20% of miners into unprofitability and forcing public miners to sell more than 32,000 BTC in Q1 to cover operating costs. Network difficulty dropped 10% in early June as higher-cost rigs powered down, and JPMorgan expects larger, more frequent adjustments while bitcoin stays near $62,500.

title: Tom Lee Says 'Zero Chance' of Ethereum Funding Crisis as Insider Warns of $30M Gap
blurb: BitMine chairman Tom Lee dismissed warnings of an Ethereum core-development funding crisis, calling the risk "zero chance" after former Foundation contributor Trent Van Epps estimated the network's 10-plus client teams and researchers cost roughly $30M a year to sustain. The dispute lands as the Ethereum Foundation deliberately shrinks its role, with co-executive director Hsiao-Wei Wang resigning amid at least eight senior departures in 2026.

title: Andre Cronje, two others resign from Sonic Labs board as S token trades 97% below its peak
blurb: Andre Cronje and two other directors resigned from the board of Sonic Labs, the Layer 1 formerly known as Fantom, with the S token trading roughly 97% below its all-time high. Sonic Labs said the three remain invested in the network's success but will no longer make business decisions for the chain.

title: Bitcoin traders load up on bearish bets all the way down to $52,000
blurb: Bitcoin options traders piled into short- and near-dated out-of-the-money puts on Deribit over the past 24 to 48 hours, with strikes spanning $61,500 down to $52,000 across June 22 to July 31 expirations. The hedging surge tracks a hawkish Fed, persistent ETF outflows, and pressure on Strategy, whose STRC preferred stock has fallen well below its $100 par value as BTC trades near $62,400.

title: Ethereum Foundation Loses Second Co-Executive Director as Hsiao-Wei Wang Steps Down
blurb: Hsiao-Wei Wang resigned as co-executive director of the Ethereum Foundation on Thursday, the second co-ED departure in four months and part of an exodus of at least eight senior figures since the spring. The turnover coincides with a funding warning from former coordinator Trent Van Epps, who estimates a roughly $30M annual gap for client teams and protocol-coordination groups after the Client Incentive Program expired in April.

title: Polymarket reportedly paid creators to post deceptive videos about fake bets
blurb: Polymarket paid online creators to post deceptive videos showing fabricated bets and winnings on near-perfect copies of its website, according to a Wall Street Journal investigation of 1,100 videos and the company's creator instructional materials. The contractor-run campaign told creators not to disclose payment until journalists started asking, and Polymarket now says it will audit its promotional content.

title: Fentanyl-linked Chinese network tied to crypto fraud from Japan involving fake ‘Zksync.jp’ token: Nikkei
blurb: A Nikkei investigation tied a fentanyl-linked Chinese criminal network to a crypto fraud operation run from Japan that distributed a fake token branded "zksync.jp" to impersonate the zkSync ecosystem. The scheme defrauded users worldwide of more than $1 million, highlighting how established trafficking networks are diversifying into token-based scams that exploit recognizable protocol names.

title: Notorious ‘jaredfromsubway’ MEV bot drained for roughly $7.5 million in counter-MEV honeypot
blurb: The jaredfromsubway MEV bot, one of Ethereum's most active sandwich-attack operators, was drained of roughly $7.5 million after interacting with a counter-MEV honeypot engineered to exploit its logic. An X account using the bot's name claimed a $15 million loss and posted a $1 million bounty, but on-chain evidence points to a likely impersonator rather than the operator.

title: Are perps swaps? A quick look at that CME suit: State of Crypto
blurb: CME Group sued the CFTC on Thursday, asking a court to vacate the agency's May approval of Kalshi's perpetual futures and arguing that perps are legally "swaps" under Dodd-Frank rather than futures, which would subject them to different regulatory requirements. The classification fight matters because crypto perps are a fast-growing share of US-listed derivatives, and a ruling could reset how prediction-market and crypto venues secure DCM approvals.

title: AWS Plugs Coinbase's x402 Into CloudFront, Letting Publishers Charge AI Agents in USDC
blurb: AWS turned on per-request AI agent monetization inside AWS WAF Bot Control, letting any site behind CloudFront charge agents in USDC on Base or Solana through Coinbase's x402 protocol, with settlement verified onchain inside a single request cycle. It is the first time a hyperscale cloud has wired onchain settlement into its content-delivery edge, and AWS takes no cut, routing disbursement straight to the publisher's self-managed wallet.

title: The Transfer Market
blurb: The author dissects Wise's business of moving money, where the core challenge is a customer base that is a patchwork of niches: remittance senders, expats with overseas mortgages, cross-border workers, and small businesses, with no single coherent audience. With 19M customers and 70% acquired by word-of-mouth, Wise is now spending upwards of 5% of revenue on marketing, including a World Cup campaign aimed at the 10M visitors who will need to buy dollars.

title: Cell-Based Architecture for Resilient Payment Systems
blurb: American Express detailed how it rebuilt its core payments platform around a cell-based architecture, grouping microservices, databases, and dependencies into independent cells that fail in isolation rather than cascading across the system. Each cell processes transactions without relying on others, which contains the blast radius of localized faults while keeping latency low and letting the platform scale capacity without enlarging its failure domain. The piece traces the design principles Amex has followed since beginning its 2018 cloud-native modernization.

title: CME Group to Sue CFTC Over Perpetual Futures Approval, Citing Dodd-Frank Swaps Definition
blurb: CME Group plans to sue the CFTC over its late-May approval of crypto perpetual futures, arguing the contracts are swaps under Dodd-Frank because their funding-rate mechanism involves two parties exchanging payments. A swap designation would impose a five-day margin period of risk instead of one day for futures, more than doubling collateral requirements and likely pushing perpetuals offshore, which threatens venues like Kalshi and Coinbase that the CFTC just cleared.

title: Secret Network’s Axelar bridge drained for $4.67 million in infinite-mint exploit that went unnoticed for seven days
blurb: An attacker exploited an infinite-mint bug in Secret Network's Axelar bridge to drain $4.67M, and the theft went undetected for seven days before Secret flagged it. About $770,000 of the stolen funds still sits in the attacker's Axelar wallet, which Axelar declined Secret's request to freeze, leaving recovery dependent on the bridge operator's cooperation.

title: Congress Strikes Housing-Bill Deal That Bans Fed CBDC Through 2030
blurb: Congressional negotiators folded a statutory ban on a Federal Reserve retail CBDC into the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, blocking any Fed-issued digital dollar through December 31, 2030. The text carves out private dollar-denominated stablecoins, slotting alongside the GENIUS Act framework and clearing a lane for issuers building on payment rails without competition from a central-bank product for years.

title: CLARITY Act Reaches Senate Floor With House Ready to Move Fast; Seven-Democrat Math Becomes the Gate
blurb: The CLARITY Act sits on the Senate calendar eligible for a floor vote, but clearing cloture requires 60 votes, leaving Republicans roughly seven Democrats short with only Gallego and Alsobrooks publicly on record, both noncommittal. The bill would assign most non-security tokens to the CFTC and route payment stablecoins to banking regulators under GENIUS, with the House ready to fast-follow if the Senate moves before August recess.

title: Schwab to join prediction markets race with S&P 500 event-based options: WSJ
blurb: Charles Schwab is partnering with Cboe Global Markets to offer binary options contracts on the S&P 500, paying a fixed amount or nothing based on where the index closes, in its first move into prediction markets. The brokerage is limiting the product to objectively verifiable financial outcomes rather than politics or sports, positioning it against retail-trading rivals Coinbase and Robinhood as they push deeper into the sector.

title: Charles Schwab is working with Cboe to launch S&P 500 ‘yes-or-no’ binary options contracts: WSJ
blurb: Charles Schwab is working with Cboe to launch S&P 500 binary options, yes-or-no contracts that pay out based on whether the index hits a set level, according to the WSJ. The move marks Schwab's first push into prediction-market-style products, putting a regulated brokerage in direct competition with Kalshi and Polymarket as event-contract trading draws CFTC scrutiny.

title: First confidential DeFi yield vault on Ethereum Launched
blurb: Zama, Morpho, and Steakhouse Financial launched the first confidential DeFi yield vault on Ethereum, using fully homomorphic encryption to hide deposit balances and transaction amounts while keeping yields earned onchain. The design lets institutions and privacy-conscious users supply capital without exposing positions to competitors or onchain surveillance, a gap that has kept regulated players away from public-chain lending.

title: TD Cowen says CME has the upper hand in lawsuit against CFTC over crypto perpetual futures
blurb: CME Group sued the CFTC over its approval of crypto perpetual futures for Kalshi and Coinbase, and TD Cowen says the exchange holds the stronger case on both procedural and substantive grounds. CME argues perps, which never expire, should be regulated as swaps rather than futures, a distinction that carries higher margin requirements and worse tax treatment and could reshape how US venues list crypto derivatives.

title: Stablecoin payments don't have to be irreversible
blurb: Stablecoin payments are not inherently irreversible, the author argues, because smart contracts can encode escrow, delayed settlement, chargebacks, dispute resolution, and spending controls directly onchain. Most payment companies today treat stablecoins purely as a settlement rail and keep business logic offchain, but the author expects more of that logic to move onchain as API-stitched wallet and card orchestration hits its limits.

title: A look at Jane Street's push to supercharge trading with AI and become a major AI investor; it invested $1B in CoreWeave in April and has a stake in Anthropic (Gregory Zuckerman/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Jane Street is pushing to embed AI across its trading operations while building a portfolio of AI bets, including a $1B investment in CoreWeave in April and a stake in Anthropic. The dual strategy positions the quant firm as both a heavy compute consumer and a financial backer of the infrastructure powering the AI buildout, blurring the line between trading shop and strategic AI investor.

title: Kentucky sues Kalshi and Polymarket
blurb: Kentucky's attorney general sued Kalshi and Polymarket, alleging their event contracts amount to unlicensed sports betting and gambling that violate state law. The suit adds Kentucky to a growing list of states challenging prediction markets' claim that CFTC oversight preempts local gaming rules, a jurisdictional fight that will shape whether event-contract platforms can operate nationally without state-by-state licensing.

title: The Stablecoin Opportunity for Sponsor Banks
blurb: Sponsor banks like The Bancorp, Sutton Bank, Lead Bank, and Cross River already power consumer fintechs like Cash App, Chime, and Affirm, and the author argues stablecoin issuance and settlement is their next natural revenue line. The case rests on these banks' existing role as the regulated rails behind embedded finance, positioning them to back fiat-redeemable stablecoins without ceding ground to crypto-native challengers.

title: Are perps swaps? A quick look at that CME suit: State of Crypto
blurb: CME Group sued the CFTC on Thursday, asking a court to vacate the agency's approval of Kalshi's perpetual futures contracts and alleging that perps are legally "swaps" under Dodd-Frank rather than futures. The classification fight matters because swaps and futures carry different regulatory regimes, and a win for CME would force the CFTC to rerun its approval process as more firms secure DCM status and race into perps.

title: Building the most AI-pilled engineering team in the world | Fiona Fung (Manager of the Claude Code and Cowork Teams)
blurb: Lenny's Newsletter interviews Fiona Fung, who manages Anthropic's Claude Code and Cowork teams and previously spent 11 years at Microsoft and started Facebook Marketplace at Meta. The conversation covers how she runs an AI-native engineering org where Anthropic engineers now ship 8x as much code per quarter as they did from 2021 to 2025, which roles AI will transform next, and how Claude routines have reshaped her work as a manager.
